title: Neuropharmacological activity of the pericarp of Passiflora edulis flavicarpa degener: putative involvement of C-glycosylflavonoids.
reference_abstract: Passiflora edulis has been used in traditional medicine as a sedative and to treat or prevent central disorders such as anxiety and insomnia. In this study, the central effects of the aqueous extract (AE), the butanolic fraction (BF), and the aqueous residual fraction (ARF) obtained from the pericarp of P. edulis flavicarpa were investigated in mice and the possible compounds involved in these putative neuropharmacologic effects were determined. AE, BF, and ARF increased the total time spent in the light compartment of the light:dark box, an anxiolytic-like effect, and AE also potentiated the hypnotic effects of ethyl ether, a sedative effect. The thin layer chromatography and high-performance liquid chromatography analysis indicated the predominance of C-glycosylflavonoids in these extracts and fractions, which were identified as isoorientin, vicenin-2, spinosin, and 6,8-di-C-glycosylchrysin.

outputs.X: Passiflora edulis flavicarpa Degener (P. edulis f.) is widely used in traditional medicine for its sedative, anxiolytic, and sleep-promoting properties, though the neuroactive compounds responsible remain poorly characterized. This study investigated the neuropharmacological effects and potential bioactive compounds from the pericarp of P. edulis f., focusing on their central nervous system activities. Dried pericarps were extracted with methanol and subjected to partitioning using n-hexane, ethyl acetate, and water fractions. The extract was evaluated for anti-anxiety, anticonvulsant, and sedative-like activities using elevated plus maze, pentylenetetrazol seizure models, and forced swim test protocols in male Swiss albino mice (n=8-10). outputs.Y: The neuropharmacological activity of aqueous extract from the pericarp of Passiflora edulis flavicarpa Deg. was evaluated in several experimental models, such as the mouse tail flick test, the rotarod and the open field test. In addition, the effects of some components isolated from the plant were investigated to determine their role on the observed activity. The results obtained indicate that the administration of the crude extract at a dose of 150 mg/kg reduces both pain threshold and motor performance (rotarod) without affecting locomotor activity. These findings suggest that P. edulis has a potential for therapeutic use against chronic pain due to its antinociceptive properties, while the effect on motor function could be related with the presence of other compounds present in this plant, which are able to modify central nervous system functions. In order to confirm these hypotheses, the most active component of the extract was purified and characterized as cinnamoylcatechin A (CCTA
outputs.Z: Passiflora edulis is a plant widely used in traditional medicine for its therapeutic properties. The aim of this study was to investigate neuropharmacological effects and molecular mechanisms involved in the treatment with P. edulis pericarp extract (PEPE). The chemical composition of PEPE was analyzed by HPLC-MS/MS, while neurobehavioral tests were conducted on mice and rats. In vivo studies showed that PEPE improved memory retention when administered intraperitoneally at doses of 100 and 200 mg/kg body weight. It also reduced anxiety behavior induced by morphine withdrawal syndrome, showing an anti-addictive effect. Behavioral results were accompanied by neurochemical changes detected using electrophysiological techniques. Results revealed that PEPE modulated neurotransmitter systems such as GABA, dopamine, serotonin, acetylcholine, noradrenaline, and glutamate through their respective receptors. These findings suggest that PEPE has potential applications for treating cognitive deficits and drug addiction.

title: The neurochemistry of agitation in Alzheimer's disease: a systematic review.
reference_abstract: To provide an up-to-date systematic review of the characteristics, methodology and findings of studies that have investigated the neurochemistry of agitation in Alzheimer's disease (AD). Electronic databases were searched for published peer-reviewed articles which provided data on any neurotransmitter system in relation to agitation in AD. Screening of titles and abstracts and data extraction from full texts were conducted in duplicate. Forty-five studies were included. Monoamines (serotonin, dopamine and noradrenaline) were most commonly investigated. A variety of methods were used to investigate the neurochemistry underlying agitation in AD and, although there were several conflicting findings, there was evidence of serotonergic deficit, relatively preserved dopaminergic function and compensatory overactivity of postsynaptic noradrenergic neurons in agitation in AD. Disruption of the dynamic balance between multiple neurotransmitter systems could impair functional neural networks involved in affective regulation and executive function. Differences in study design and methodology may have contributed to conflicting findings. Future studies that overcome these limitations (e.g. using standardized criteria to define agitation) and employ neuroimaging methods such as MRI/PET to investigate specific neural networks are needed to clarify the role of neurotransmitter alterations in these patients.

outputs.X: Agitation is an important clinical symptom that may occur in patients with mild to moderate Alzheimer's disease (AD). Although the mechanisms underlying this behavior remain unclear, neurochemical abnormalities have been implicated. This study examined the evidence for specific neurotransmitter systems involved in agitation in AD using a systematic literature review. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of peer-reviewed studies published between 1990-2004 addressing aggression and agitation in AD. Data were extracted from 28 eligible reports involving 1,286 patients. Aggressive behaviors occurred in 54% of participants, with 26% exhibiting severe episodes requiring physical restraint. Dopaminergic dysfunction was most consistently associated with aggressive behavior, with high levels of dopamine-D2 receptors correlated with increased risk of aggression. Serotonin abnormalities also emerged as significant factors, with decreased 5-HT2A receptor density linked to agitated behavior. Cholinergic system activity showed mixed results, with some studies suggesting reduced acetylcholine release while others found elevated cholinesterase levels.
outputs.Y: Agitation represents one of the most challenging behavioral symptoms observed in patients with Alzheimer's disease (AD), affecting up to 60% of individuals and significantly impacting caregiver burden. Despite its clinical importance, the underlying neurochemical mechanisms remain poorly understood. This systematic review examined current evidence regarding neurotransmitter systems involved in agitation in AD populations. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of published studies from 2000-2011 that investigated dopamine, serotonin, GABA, acetylcholine, and norepinephrine levels or receptor occupancy in agitated versus non-agitated AD patients using positron emission tomography (PET) or single-photon emission computed tomography (SPECT). Additionally, we evaluated pharmacological interventions targeting specific neurotransmitter systems for their efficacy in reducing agitation severity. Our analysis included data from 24 peer-reviewed studies involving 1,252 AD patients across multiple care settings. Results revealed significant reductions in prefrontal dopaminergic transmission (D2/D3 receptor availability decreased by 28%)
outputs.Z: Agitation is an often overlooked symptom of dementia and can be difficult to manage. The aim of this study was to systematically review the literature regarding the association between cognitive function, pharmacological interventions, neurotransmitter alterations, and behavioural disturbances in patients with Alzheimer's disease (AD) presenting with agitation. A comprehensive search strategy was used to identify relevant articles published until March 2015. Studies were included if they met criteria for inclusion; these were related to clinical or neuropathological aspects of AD, neurological symptoms, and the relationship between neuropsychopharmacology, brain chemistry, and behaviour. Only four studies met our selection criteria and all were based on retrospective analyses of medical records. Of those studies, two identified an association between agitation and low levels of choline acetyltransferase activity and serotonin transporter density. One study found that patients who received psychotropic drugs had higher levels of cerebrospinal fluid monoamine metabolites compared with non-psychotropic-treated patients. title: Pharmacotherapies for alcoholism: the old and the new.
reference_abstract: Alcoholism and other alcohol use disorders are major public health problems, and the success rates of non-pharmacological treatment of these disorders such as psychotherapy, cognitive-behavioral therapy, group therapy, or residential treatment programs,remain only modest at best. High rates of recidivism (relapse) in alcoholics attempting to remain abstinent are prevalent worldwide. In recent years abundant evidence has accumulated demonstrating that alcoholism is a complex and multifaceted disease of the brain caused by numerous genetic, neurobiological, developmental, environmental, and socioeconomic factors that are still not yet fully understood.There is thus a great need to improve the success rates of all forms of treatment of alcoholism not only in preventing relapse, but curbing active alcohol consumption and craving. The development of improved pharmacotherapies that could be used as adjuncts to the aforementioned non-pharmacological treatment approaches is one avenue of great interest to the scientific community and the general public. Currently there are only three medications approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for use in the treatment of alcohol abuse and alcoholism--disulfiram, naltrexone, and acamprosate. Yet medication compliance issues and the modest efficacy of these compounds leave substantial room for improvement. This special issue is devoted to reviewing the current status of these FDA approved medications in the treatment of alcoholism. In addition, preclinical and clinical evidence suggesting that other classes of medications might also be of potential use are reviewed, including anticonvulsants, GABAB receptor agonists, cholinergic receptor partial agonists, corticotropin-releasing factor and cannabinoid CB1 receptor antagonists, nociceptin receptor ligands, and the novel antipsychotic aripiprazole.

outputs.X: The pharmacological treatment of alcohol use disorder has evolved significantly over recent decades, with emerging therapeutic options offering enhanced efficacy compared to traditional agents. This comprehensive review examines current evidence-based interventions alongside novel approaches in development. We analyzed published clinical trials from 2000-2014 across major pharmacotherapy modalities including naltrexone, acamprosate, topiramate, disulfiram, and naloxone. Additionally, we evaluated preclinical data on glutamate modulators, serotonin reuptake inhibitors, and neuroprotective compounds targeting acute withdrawal symptoms. Naltrexone demonstrated consistent efficacy in reducing heavy drinking episodes (effect size d=0.43) through reduced craving and altered reward processing mechanisms. Acamprosate showed modest but significant benefits in relapse prevention (d=0.27), particularly when combined with behavioral therapy. Topiramate provided additional advantages as a dual-action anti-craving agent with anticonvulsant properties. Disulfiram's efficacy remains limited
outputs.Y: The therapeutic approaches to alcoholism have not changed significantly over the past 20 years. This review examines current pharmacological treatments of alcohol use disorder (AUD) based on clinical evidence from randomized controlled trials or meta-analyses, as well as recent advances in understanding mechanisms of drug action. There is now strong evidence that naltrexone reduces heavy drinking by approximately 30% in patients with AUD who are seeking treatment. The mechanism involves an effect on motivation and reinforcement processing rather than a direct opioid receptor blocking property. Nalmefene, a selective opioid antagonist, has been shown to be effective in reducing alcohol consumption when administered shortly after a drink. The drugs acamprosate and topiramate appear to reduce relapse rates following treatment for alcohol dependence but do so at the cost of serious adverse effects. The results of studies involving disulfiram, naltrexone and acamprosate suggest that these medications may be most effective when used together. There is no clear evidence that
outputs.Z: The first pharmacotherapy approved in the USA for the treatment of alcohol dependence was naltrexone, which is a partial mu-opioid receptor antagonist. It has been shown to be effective when used as part of a combination therapy consisting of an opioid antagonist combined with cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT). Nalmefene, a more potent opioid antagonist than naltrexone, was approved by the European Medicines Agency in 2009 for use in alcohol-dependent patients who have already been treated with naltrexone. Acamprosate, a glutamate receptor modulator, was introduced in the early 1990s. The evidence supporting its efficacy is still being debated, but it is likely that acamprosate can reduce relapse rates among heavy drinkers when used as part of a combination treatment approach. title: Neuropharmacology of the essential oil of bergamot.
reference_abstract: Bergamot (Citrus bergamia, Risso) is a fruit most knowledgeable for its essential oil (BEO) used in aromatherapy to minimize symptoms of stress-induced anxiety and mild mood disorders and cancer pain though the rational basis for such applications awaits to be discovered. The behavioural and EEG spectrum power effects of BEO correlate well with its exocytotic and carrier-mediated release of discrete amino acids endowed with neurotransmitter function in the mammalian hippocampus supporting the deduction that BEO is able to interfere with normal and pathological synaptic plasticity. The observed neuroprotection in the course of experimental brain ischemia and pain does support this view. In conclusion, the data yielded so far contribute to our understanding of the mode of action of this phytocomplex on nerve tissue under normal and pathological experimental conditions and provide a rational basis for the practical use of BEO in complementary medicine. The opening of a wide venue for future research and translation into clinical settings is also envisaged.

outputs.X: Bergamot (Citrus aurantium) essential oil has been traditionally used for cognitive enhancement and mood elevation, yet its neuropharmacological mechanisms remain poorly understood. This study investigated the effects of bergamot essential oil on neurotransmitter systems in rat brain tissue using in vitro microdialysis and ex vivo autoradiography techniques. Male Sprague-Dawley rats received intraperitoneal injections of bergamot essential oil (50, 100, or 200 mg/kg) or vehicle control. In microdialysis experiments, real-time monitoring of dopamine, serotonin, norepinephrine, acetylcholine, and GABA concentrations was performed in the prefrontal cortex, hippocampus, striatum, and nucleus accumbens. Ex vivo autoradiography was employed to quantify dopamine D2 receptor binding sites. Bergamot essential oil dose-dependently increased extracellular dopamine levels by 36%, 62%, and 84% in the prefrontal cortex
outputs.Y: The bergamot fruit (Citrus bergamia Risso & Poit.) is a small citrus fruit, native to Calabria region in Italy. The essential oil obtained from its peel has been traditionally used in medicine for its antioxidant and antimicrobial activities. In addition, it exhibits numerous pharmacological properties such as antidepressive effects, analgesic activity, neuroprotective action against Alzheimer's disease and Parkinson's disease. However, the mechanisms underlying these actions are still poorly understood. The present study was designed to investigate the neuropharmacological profile of the essential oil of bergamot. We evaluated the effects of this essential oil on brain function in mice using behavioural tests including locomotor activity, anxiety-like behaviour and learning and memory processes. The results showed that oral administration of the essential oil significantly increased locomotor activity and decreased anxiety-like behaviour without affecting spontaneous motor activity. Furthermore, the essential oil improved spatial working memory performance in an eight-arm radial maze task. Finally, the essential oil exert
outputs.Z: The present review analyzes the neurochemical properties of the essential oil of Bergamot, a product from Italy. The essential oil was obtained by hydrodistillation from the peels of Citrus aurantium var. Bergamia and analyzed for its chemical composition. In particular, it is reported that the major constituents are the diterpenes nerolidol (32%), limonene (22%) and neral (18%). Further studies were conducted on behavioral and pharmacological effects in vivo of the essential oil, as well as in vitro on binding to specific receptors or enzymatic activities. It has been demonstrated that the essential oil could be useful in treating anxiety disorders and depression due to its anxiolytic activity. Moreover, it may have a positive effect on cognitive functions because of its cholinergic and anticholinesterase properties. Furthermore, it can be used as antidepressant and anti-anxiety agent with no addictive potential. Finally, this review shows some evidence about other interesting pharmacological

title: Looking at neurotransmitters in the microscope.
reference_abstract: This review article covers the early period of my career. I first summarize research initiated by the late Nils-Ake Hillarp, after his appointment in 1962 as professor in the Department of Histology at Karolinska Institutet. He only lived for three more years, but during this short period he started up a group of ten students who explored various aspects of the three monoamine transmitters, dopamine, noradrenaline and 5-hydroxytryptamine, using the new formaldehyde fluorescence method developed by Bengt Falck and Hillarp in Lund. This method allowed visualization of the cellular localization in the microscope of these monoamines, which introduced a new discipline in neurobiology-chemical neuroanatomy. I then deal with work aiming at localizing the monoamines at the ultrastructural level, as well as attempts to use radioactively labeled aminoacids, especially gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA), and autoradiography, to identify, in the microscope, neurons using such transmitters. Finally, our immunohistochemical work together with Kjell Fuxe and the late Menek Goldstein, using antibodies to four monoamine-synthesizing enzymes is summarized, including some aspects on the adrenaline neurons, which had escaped detection with the Falck-Hillarp technique.

outputs.X: The visualization of neurotransmitter dynamics in living neural tissue remains a significant challenge in neuropharmacology research. Traditional approaches utilizing fluorescent probes often suffer from poor spatial resolution and temporal limitations, limiting our ability to observe real-time neurotransmission events within single neurons or synaptic contacts. We developed a novel optical imaging technique combining two-photon excitation with fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) detection to visualize dopamine release in hippocampal neurons using an engineered yellow fluorescent protein (YFP)-dopamine complex. Primary cultures of CA1 pyramidal cells were prepared and loaded with YFP-dopaamide conjugates through patch-clamped electrophysiological recording chambers. Two-photon microscopy was used to image FRET signals between donor YFP and acceptor cyan fluorescent proteins (CFP), enabling quantitative assessment of dopaminergic activity during action potential firing and pharmacological manipulation. This approach demonstrated unprecedented subcellular spatial resolution (0.2-0.5 μm) and temporal precision (<10 ms
outputs.Y: The use of neurochemistry to understand brain function has a long history. The most widely used techniques for studying the neurochemical basis of behavior are those that determine concentrations of specific chemicals, such as dopamine or serotonin, and how they change under different conditions. These methods have provided valuable information about the effects of drugs and disease on brain chemistry but have given only limited insight into the details of how these substances interact with their receptors and other target molecules. Recently, there has been a great deal of interest in using imaging techniques to study this process, since it is possible to watch what happens when a single molecule binds to its receptor rather than waiting days or weeks until the effect can be measured by chemical analysis. In this article we describe some recent advances in microscopes that allow us to see inside living cells and how these tools can be used to learn more about how the human brain works. We focus primarily on the use of fluorescent probes to visualize neurotransmitter release, but also discuss the use of light-based molecular genetics
outputs.Z: The first time I saw a real live cell was in 1970. The cell was an intact neurone and it looked exactly like what we had imagined from our neuroanatomical studies of neurons and their synapses. I remember being excited but also disappointed because the cells did not appear to be doing anything interesting. It took another decade before my enthusiasm for neuroscience really began to grow, and that was when we started using microscopes to look inside living nervous systems. In this article I will briefly review some recent developments in optical methods used to monitor nerve activity in whole animals and in cultured neurons, including conventional fluorescence microscopy, confocal laser scanning microscopy, two-photon excitation microscopy, and multiphoton microscopy. These techniques have made possible many advances in understanding neural function, particularly during development, in the brain, and in sensory organs. title: Psychiatric safety of ketamine in psychopharmacology research.
reference_abstract: A growing number of investigators are studying ketamine effects in healthy human subjects, but concerns remain about its safety as a research tool. Therefore, it is timely to revisit the safety of subanesthetic doses of ketamine in experimental psychopharmacology studies. To report on the safety of laboratory studies with subanesthetic doses of ketamine in healthy humans using an existing dataset. Medically healthy subjects with no personal or familial Axis I psychotic spectrum disorders were administered subanesthetic doses of ketamine by intravenous infusion in a series of clinical investigations from 1989 to 2005. The safety of ketamine administration was monitored in these subjects. Four hundred and fifty subjects received at least one dose of active ketamine. Eight hundred and thirty three active ketamine and 621 placebo infusions were administered. Ten adverse mental status events were documented in nine subjects/infusions that were deemed related to ketamine administration (2% of subjects, 1.45% of infusions). All but one adverse reaction resolved by the end of the test session. The side effects in the remaining individual were no longer clinically significant within 4 days of the test session. No residual sequelae were observed. Ketamine administration at subanesthetic doses appears to present an acceptable level of risk for carefully screened populations of healthy human subjects in the context of clinical research programs that intensively monitor subjects throughout their study participation.

outputs.X: Ketamine, an N-methyl-d-aspartate receptor antagonist, has been used as a pharmacological tool to investigate the neurobiology of mood disorders. In this review we highlight recent studies investigating the effects of single and repeated doses of ketamine on psychiatric symptoms in healthy volunteers, with focus on negative mood states such as depression and anxiety. There is evidence that ketamine produces transient reductions in depressive symptomatology and anxiety. The mechanisms by which these effects are produced remain unclear but may be due to activation of endogenous opioid systems or alterations in neurotransmitter activity within the central nervous system. Single doses have not yet been shown to produce lasting changes in mood, however repeated administration appears to induce some long-lasting cognitive deficits. We conclude that while there are promising data supporting the use of ketamine as a therapeutic agent for depressed patients, further research into its potential risks and benefits are needed before it can be recommended as a treatment option. Such work should include consideration of the possible consequences of short-term and chronic
outputs.Y: Ketamine has emerged as a promising investigational agent for treating treatment-resistant depression and anxiety disorders, yet its psychiatric side effects remain poorly characterized. This study evaluated the acute and delayed cognitive, mood, and behavioral adverse effects of ketamine in healthy volunteers to inform safe use in clinical research settings. Forty-four young adults (ages 18-30 years) received either placebo or 0.5 mg/kg intravenous ketamine during structured laboratory sessions over two consecutive days. Psychometric assessments included neurocognitive testing, mood scales, personality questionnaires, and observational measures of behavior and physiology at baseline, 2 hours post-administration, 24 hours later, and 7 days after injection. Ketamine produced significant dose-dependent reductions in negative affect scores on the Positive and Negative Affect Schedule and increased positive affect by day 2 compared to placebo (p<0.01). Cognitive performance remained stable across time points except for small but statistically significant declines in working memory efficiency. Personality traits revealed mild alterations
outputs.Z: Ketamine has emerged as a potent and rapidly acting antidepressant agent, yet its clinical use remains limited due to potential psychiatric adverse effects. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of ketamine-induced behavioral alterations across multiple experimental paradigms to evaluate its psychopharmacological profile and predict potential risks for clinical application. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=96) were administered intraperitoneal injections of 10-30 mg/kg ketamine or vehicle controls at various time points (0, 5, 15, 30 minutes). Behavioral assessments included open-field activity, spontaneous locomotor behavior, novel object recognition, social interaction tests, and operant conditioning. Neuroimaging studies using magnetic resonance spectroscopy were performed during acute administration. Ketamine treatment significantly altered both exploratory behavior and social interactions within the first hour post-injection, with maximal effects occurring at 20-25mg/kg doses. title: Neurobiology and neuroimmunology of Tourette's syndrome: an update.
reference_abstract: Tourette's syndrome is a childhood-onset neuropsychiatric disorder characterized by the presence of both multiple motor and vocal tics. While the pathogenesis at a molecular and cellular level remains unknown, structural and functional neuroimaging studies point to the involvement of the basal ganglia and related cortico-striato-thalamo-cortical circuits as the neuroanatomical site for Tourette's syndrome. Moreover, Tourette's syndrome has a strong genetic component, and considerable progress has been made in understanding the mode of transmission and in identifying potential genomic loci. Summaries of recent findings in these areas will be reviewed, followed by a critical overview of findings both supporting and challenging the proposed autoimmune hypothesis of Tourette's syndrome. We conclude that Tourette's syndrome is a heterogeneous disorder, and that immune factors may indeed be involved in some patients.

outputs.X: Tourette's syndrome (TS) represents a complex neuropsychiatric disorder characterized by motor and vocal tics, with underlying pathophysiology likely involving both genetic and environmental factors. While previous research has focused primarily on the behavioral manifestations of TS, emerging evidence suggests significant neurobiological mechanisms may contribute to symptom onset and progression. This review synthesizes recent advances in our understanding of TS neurobiology, emphasizing findings from neuroimaging studies, electrophysiological investigations, and neurotransmitter systems implicated in tic formation. Neuroimaging studies have revealed altered dopaminergic activity in striatal regions, particularly the caudate nucleus and putamen, suggesting dysregulation of reward processing mechanisms. Electrophysiological recordings demonstrate abnormal sensorimotor gating abnormalities, particularly in prefrontal-striatal circuits, supporting dysfunction in executive control over involuntary movements. Additionally, increased levels of glutamate, GABA, and acetylcholine in specific brain areas have been identified, suggesting multisystem involvement beyond traditional mono
outputs.Y: Tourette's disorder (TD) is a neuropsychiatric condition characterized by multiple motor and vocal tics that may persist for years or decades. Despite its prevalence, the underlying neurobiological mechanisms remain poorly understood. We conducted a comprehensive review of current knowledge regarding TD pathophysiology, focusing on neurotransmitter systems, immune system involvement, and genetic factors. Neurotransmitters implicated in TD include dopamine, serotonin, acetylcholine, noradrenaline, GABA, glutamate, and neuropeptides such as substance P and somatostatin. Dopaminergic hyperactivity has been suggested through studies showing elevated dopamine concentrations in striatal regions, including the caudate nucleus, putamen, and substantia nigra pars compacta. Serotonergic dysfunction has also been proposed based on findings of reduced 5-HT2A receptor binding and increased serotonergic metabolites in cerebrospinal fluid. Glutamatergic modulation appears to play a critical role in tic
outputs.Z:
Tourette's syndrome (TS) is a neuropsychiatric disorder characterized by the presence of multiple motor tics and one or more vocal tics, which can be present in any combination. TS is one of the most common neurodevelopmental disorders that affects about 0.3% of children and adolescents worldwide. It has been hypothesized that TS is caused by genetic factors; however, there are still no definite genes identified for TS to date. In this review article, we will discuss recent advances made in the field of neurobiological and immunological mechanisms underlying the pathogenesis of TS with special attention given to its clinical manifestations. We also address the need for further investigations into potential pharmacotherapeutic interventions as well as novel treatment strategies including stem cell therapy and gene therapy. Finally, we conclude our discussion on the current state-of-the-art knowledge regarding the pathophysiology of TS and its future direction. ### Keywords:

title: Traditional Chinese medicine and Western psychopharmacology: building bridges.
reference_abstract: This paper demonstrates that in the treatment of psychiatric disorders, there are striking similarities between the mechanisms of psychoactive agents used in Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM) and those of western psychopharmacology. While western researchers search for new treatments and novel mechanisms of action, investigators in Asia are analyzing traditional remedies in order to understand the mechanisms responsible for their effectiveness. A review of contemporary pharmacologic studies of agents used in TCM for psychiatric indications reveals that virtually all of the active principles of drug action established in 20th century psychopharmacology were encountered empirically in Chinese herbal medicine over the past 2000 years. Building bridges between these two traditions may thus be of benefit to both cultures. In addition to providing western patients with a wider selection of treatment options, the effort may help Asian clinicians and researchers avoid some of the errors that have troubled their western counterparts.

outputs.X: Traditional Chinese medicine (TCM) has emerged as a complementary therapeutic approach for psychiatric disorders, yet limited scientific evidence exists regarding its pharmacological mechanisms. This study investigated the neuropharmacological interactions between TCM compounds and conventional antidepressants to identify potential synergistic or additive effects in treating major depressive disorder. We conducted a double-blind, placebo-controlled crossover trial involving 60 patients with moderate-to-severe depression randomized to receive either fluoxetine (20 mg/d) plus traditional herbal formula (HF; Gao-Yin-Tang), fluoxetine alone, or HF alone for 8 weeks. Primary outcome measures included Hamilton Depression Rating Scale scores and plasma levels of serotonin metabolites. Secondary endpoints assessed cognitive function, quality-of-life parameters, and adverse event profiles across treatment periods. Results demonstrated significant improvement in depressive symptoms following both treatment groups compared to baseline (p<0.001). outputs.Y: The traditional Chinese medical system has developed over thousands of years, with its own unique therapeutic approaches to mental health disorders. While modern Western pharmacological treatments have proven effective for many psychiatric conditions, there remains a growing interest in integrating Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM) practices with conventional psychotherapy. This review examines the evidence supporting such integrative approaches from the perspective of both Eastern and Western clinical practice. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of published studies investigating TCM therapies for anxiety, depression, schizophrenia, and addiction. Our findings indicate that while some specific herbs may show promise for treating certain symptoms, their effectiveness is often modest at best. Moreover, there are significant concerns regarding safety profiles and potential interactions with western medications. Current research suggests that more rigorous trials using standardized preparations and systematic evaluations of efficacy versus placebo controls are needed before TCM interventions can be recommended as standard treatment options. Nevertheless, integration of TCM principles into existing psychological therapy programs shows considerable potential for enhancing patient outcomes across diverse populations worldwide. outputs.Z: The development of traditional Chinese medicine (TCM) over the past few thousand years has produced a rich pharmacopeia that includes more than 5,000 substances used in clinical practice. TCM is based on the concept of "syndromes", which are related to the physical and mental health of patients; they represent a system for diagnosing disease, prescribing medicines, and treating illness. In recent decades, there have been increasing interactions between western medicine and TCM. This review summarizes some key concepts from TCM and discusses the potential benefits of using them in the treatment of psychiatric disorders. It also highlights the challenges involved in integrating these two systems of healthcare delivery and explains how this integration can be achieved. Finally, it presents the future direction of research into the application of TCM-based therapies for mental illnesses. title: Acute treatment of migraine headaches.
reference_abstract: Optimum acute treatment of migraine requires prevention of headache as a top priority. Recognition of the multitude of migraine presentations, the frequency of total headache attacks, and number of days of headache disability are critical. Successful treatment requires excellent patient-clinician communication enhancing confidence and mutual trust based on patient needs and preferences. Optimum management of acute migraine nearly always requires pharmacologic treatment for rapid resolution. Migraine-specific triptans, dihydroergotamine, and several antiinflammatories have substantial empirical clinical efficacy. Older nonspecific drugs, particularly butalbital and opioids, contribute to medication overuse headache and are to be avoided. Clinicians should utilize evidence-based acute migraine-specific therapy stressing the imperative acute treatment goal of early intervention, but not too often with the correct drug, formulation, and dose. This therapy needs to provide cost-effective fast results, meaningful to the patient while minimizing the need for additional drugs. Migraine-ACT evaluates 2-hour pain freedom with return to normal function, comfort with treatment, and consistency of response. Employ a thoroughly educated patient, formulary, testimonials, stratification, and rational cotherapy against the race to central sensitization for optimum outcomes.

outputs.X: Migraine headaches represent a significant burden on public health systems worldwide, affecting approximately 12% of the general population. Traditional acute treatments including triptans and ergotamines remain first-line therapies despite limited efficacy in certain patient populations. This study evaluated the therapeutic potential of novel compounds targeting trigeminovascular pathways for acute migraine relief. We conducted a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial involving 240 patients presenting with moderate-to-severe migraine attacks. Participants received either sumatriptan 100 mg (n=80), eletriptan 40 mg (n=80) or placebo (n=80). Primary outcome measures included pain reduction at 2 hours post-dose assessed using visual analog scales and headache freedom rates by 4-hour follow-up. Secondary endpoints examined tolerability profiles and pharmacokinetic parameters. outputs.Y: Migraine is a prevalent and debilitating neurologic disorder characterized by recurrent moderate to severe headaches associated with autonomic symptoms such as photophobia, phonophobia, nausea, and vomiting. Current pharmacologic therapies for acute treatment of migraines include triptans (sumatriptan, rizatriptan), nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (naproxen), and ergotamine derivatives. While these medications provide effective pain relief in many patients, they can also cause significant side effects including dizziness, drowsiness, and cardiovascular events. In this review article we examine the recent clinical trial data regarding new therapeutic approaches that have emerged over the past few years for acute treatment of migraines. We focus on evidence-based studies published between 1998-2003 that examined both novel drug classes and established treatments from other medical disciplines. New compounds that are currently being investigated include selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (fluoxetine), anticonvulsants (topiramate), beta-blockers (pro
outputs.Z: The majority of patients with acute migraine attacks can be treated effectively at home, but some require hospitalization. The first priority is to establish a diagnosis, which includes determining the cause and location of the headache (migraine or tension-type), its severity, duration, and any preceding symptoms such as visual disturbance. Next, an assessment of medical status is made to determine if there are concomitant conditions that need to be addressed, especially those requiring emergency intervention. Once these needs have been met, the patient should be moved to a private room so they do not disturb other patients, families, or staff. Patients may be given oral medications prior to admission for pain control; however, intravenous medication may also be required. In this chapter, we review the management strategies used in hospitals to treat severe migraines. title: The psychopharmacology of violence: making sensible decisions.
reference_abstract: Violent behavior associated with mental disorders is a common reason for admission to a psychiatric inpatient unit. Once hospitalized, patients may continue to be intermittently agitated and have persistent aggressive behaviors, preventing their discharge back into the community. Managing agitation quickly with effective pharmacological agents can avoid further escalation to aggression and violence. In the acute setting, this usually involves the parenteral use of antipsychotics, with or without benzodiazepines. Within the past decade, short-acting intramuscular formulations of second-generation antipsychotics have become available and provide a means to induce calm with a substantially lower risk of acute dystonia or akathisia compared with haloperidol. New alternative formulations that avoid injections include inhalation and sublingual administration. Longer-term management of persistent aggressive behavior by reducing the frequency and intensity of future episodes of agitation is more complex. In contrast to agitation associated with schizophrenia or bipolar mania, no agents have yet been approved by regulatory agencies for the treatment of persistent aggressive behavior. The strongest evidence supports the use of clozapine as an antihostility agent, followed by olanzapine. Adjunctive strategies with anticonvulsants and beta-adrenergic agents may also be worthwhile to consider.

outputs.X: Violent behavior represents a complex phenomenon with underlying neurobiological, psychological, and sociocultural components that necessitate thoughtful pharmacotherapeutic interventions. This review synthesizes current evidence regarding the therapeutic use of psychotropic medications in individuals with violent tendencies while considering relevant safety considerations. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of controlled trials, case series, and observational studies published between 2000-2012 examining antipsychotics, mood stabilizers, and other psychiatric medications in patients with history of aggression or violence. Our systematic literature search yielded 78 relevant articles, including 29 randomized controlled trials investigating atypical antipsychotics in aggressive disorders. Results demonstrated significant reductions in violent behaviors when compared to placebo in 14 out of 22 studies evaluating risperidone (p<0.001) and olanzapine (p<0.01). Lithium showed efficacy for impulsive aggression but required careful monitoring due to potential cardiac risks. Antidepressants appeared less effective for primary aggressivity and may
outputs.Y:
Although the use of psychotropic drugs to prevent or reduce violent behavior has been practiced for many years, there is still much confusion and controversy. The purpose of this article is to describe current evidence regarding the effectiveness of pharmacological interventions in preventing or reducing violent behavior. In addition, recommendations are made about how clinicians should make decisions about whether to prescribe psychotropic medications for these purposes. The most recent data indicate that the efficacy of psychotropics is generally limited. However, when other treatments have failed, they may be useful in certain cases. Nevertheless, clinicians must proceed with caution because of the potential side effects, adverse interactions with other agents, and possibility of drug misuse. Finally, we discuss the need for additional research to better identify those patients who might benefit from such treatment. This information will aid clinical decision-making as well as provide a basis for future research efforts. (PsycINFO Database Record (c) 2007 APA, all rights reserved). ### Abstract:

outputs.Z: Psychotropic medications are often prescribed to individuals with violent behavior or aggressive tendencies. However, the effectiveness and safety profile of these drugs in controlling violent behaviors remain poorly understood. This review examines current evidence regarding the efficacy of antipsychotics, antidepressants, and mood stabilizers for treating violent behavior and aggression. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of randomized controlled trials (RCTs) and observational studies published between 2000-2013 focusing on patients with violent history who were treated with psychotropic medication. We analyzed data from over 1,000 participants across multiple treatment conditions including antipsychotic monotherapy, combination therapy, and behavioral interventions. Overall, our findings indicate that while some patients may benefit from specific classes of psychotropics such as anticonvulsants or atypical neuroleptics, there is currently insufficient evidence to support widespread use of any single drug class. title: Impairments in reflective cognitive functions in alcoholics: a neuropharmacological model.
reference_abstract: The ability to make use of reflective cognitive operations in monitoring and evaluating remembered events is impaired in subgroups of nominally cognitively unimpaired, detoxified alcoholics. Alcoholics, relative to controls, make more errors in identifying the source of remembered information (i.e. whether a remembered word was self-generated or was a stimulus word presented by the experimenter), and are impaired in their ability to inhibit confabulatory errors (intrusions). The cognitive-memory impairment expressed in benzodiazepine-treated normal volunteers mimics this impairment in alcoholics. Disturbances in prefrontal and frontal lobe functions may be involved in this selective impairment in cognition in many alcoholics and may also contribute to what accounts for the failures in reflective cognitive operations observed in amnestic patients.

outputs.X: Recent research has demonstrated that alcoholism is characterized by deficits in several aspects of cognition, including attentional processes. However, the nature and mechanisms of these impairments are not fully understood. In this review, we discuss recent findings regarding the effects of alcohol on attention, memory, planning, decision-making, conflict resolution, executive control, working memory, and emotion processing. We also consider how these deficits may relate to other symptoms of alcohol dependence, such as depression, anxiety, and impulsivity. Finally, we present an integrative model of the neural basis of these cognitive deficits in alcoholism, which draws upon the concept of reward-based learning. This model suggests that alterations in neuronal pathways involved in reward-based learning contribute to the cognitive deficiencies seen in alcoholics. These alterations include changes in cortical structures (e.g., prefrontal cortex), subcortical regions (e.g., amygdala), and peripheral systems (e.g., endocrine). outputs.Y: Alcoholism is characterized by persistent cognitive deficits that significantly impact treatment compliance and long-term recovery outcomes. Recent evidence suggests that these impairments may result from altered neurotransmitter systems, particularly those regulating reflective cognitive processes such as working memory, attentional control, and self-reflection. This study investigated the neuropharmacological mechanisms underlying reflective dysfunction in individuals with alcohol use disorders (AUD). We conducted a double-blind, placebo-controlled crossover trial involving 48 patients in early stages of AUD (Gordon Diagnostic Scale score ≤7) and age-matched controls. Participants received either escitalopram (20 mg/day), naltrexone (50 mg/day), or combination therapy while completing standardized neuropsychological assessments measuring executive function domains including Tower of London, Wisconsin Card Sorting Test, and modified Trail Making Test. Blood samples were collected for pharmacokinetic analysis of both medications. outputs.Z: Alcohol dependence is characterized by multiple impairments in executive function, including impaired planning and attention. While some of these deficits may be due to chronic brain damage, it remains unclear how other executive dysfunction emerges during acute intoxication. We tested the hypothesis that alcohols impair reflective thinking, a higher-order executive function involving meta-cognitive processes that are thought to modulate response selection. Participants (N=80) were recruited from a university community. They performed a modified Stroop task, a verbal fluency test, a letter-number sequencing task, and an implicit association task while under the influence of 0.08% blood-alcohol concentration. Alcohol impairment was quantified using the National Institute on Alcohol Abuse and Alcoholism's Clinical Guidelines for Alcohol Intervention and Treatment. Performance on all tasks was significantly impaired at every level of intoxication relative to baseline performance. These effects emerged as early as.03% blood-alcohol concentration. title: Therapeutic potential of vanilloid receptor TRPV1 agonists and antagonists as analgesics: Recent advances and setbacks.
reference_abstract: The vanilloid receptor TRPV1 is a homotetrameric, non-selective cation channel abundantly expressed in the nociceptors (c-fibers). TRPV1 is considered as a highly validated pain target because, i) its agonists such as capsaicin cause desensitization of TRPV1 channels that relieves pain behaviors in preclinical species, and ii) its antagonists relieve pain behaviors in rodent models of inflammation, osteoarthritis, and cancer. Hence, both agonists and antagonists of TRPV1 are being evaluated as potential analgesics in clinical trials. Clinical trial results of TRPV1 agonists such as resiniferatoxin in interstitial cystitis, NGX 4010 in post-herpetic neuralgia, and 4975 (Adlea) in osteoarthritis, bunionectomy, and Morton's neuroma have been reported. Similarly, clinical trial results of TRPV1 antagonists such as SB-705498 and AMG 517 have also been published recently. Overall, some molecules (e.g., capsaicin) demonstrated potential analgesia in certain conditions (postsurgical pain, postherpetic neuralgia, pain in diabetic neuropathy, osteoarthritis, bunionectomy, and Morton's neuroma), whereas others fell out of the clinic due to on-target liabilities or failed to demonstrate efficacy. This review summarizes recent advances and setbacks of TRPV1 agonists and antagonists in the clinic and predicts future directions.

outputs.X: The transient receptor potential channel vanilloid type 1 (TRPV1) is a polymodal cation-selective nonselective ion channel, which has been shown to be involved in nociception. The activation of the TRPV1 channels by capsaicin or protons results in an increase in the intracellular calcium concentration through its association with phospholipase C. This leads to the release of neurotransmitters such as calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP). Therefore, the presence of this protein could represent a new therapeutic target for the treatment of pain and inflammation. There are two main classes of compounds that interact directly with the TRPV1 channel: one class consists of activators and another of inhibitors. In addition, there have been studies on the use of selective TRPV1 modulators in different models of inflammatory pain. Although there have been promising results using these drugs, further research is required to determine their efficacy and safety profile in human patients. In this review we
outputs.Y: The transient receptor potential vanilloid type 1 (TRPV1) channel has emerged as a promising therapeutic target for chronic pain management due to its role in nociceptive transduction. Both TRPV1 agonists and antagonists have shown significant promise in preclinical studies, yet clinical translation remains limited by off-target effects and poor oral bioavailability. This review examines recent advances in TRPV1-based analgesic development, evaluating the efficacy and safety profiles of lead compounds from phase I-III clinical trials published between 2008-2012. We analyzed data from 12 completed or ongoing human studies using capsaicin analogs, synthetic capsacinoids, and selective TRPV1 modulators including AMG9810, LY2940680, and SB408124. While initial results with capsaicin derivatives showed modest efficacy in osteoarthritis patients, subsequent trials with optimized formulations demonstrated improved tolerability profiles. outputs.Z: Vanilloid receptor type 1 (TRPV1) is a non-selective cation channel that has been identified in nociceptors. The activation of this channel leads to the release of pro-inflammatory mediators, such as cytokines, nitric oxide, and prostaglandins, which are involved in the transmission of pain signals. Thus, it is not surprising that the inhibition of TRPV1 activity results in reduced pain perception and hyperalgesia. In the past few years, many natural compounds have been found to inhibit TRPV1 activity through different mechanisms, including direct binding to its ligand-binding domain or indirect binding via conformational changes and modulation of intracellular signaling pathways. These molecules include capsaicinoids, curcuminoids, resveratrol, phytosterols, triterpenes, quercetin, flavonols, phenylpropanoids, etc., among others. title: Cholinergic effects on fear conditioning I: the degraded contingency effect is disrupted by atropine but reinstated by physostigmine.
reference_abstract: The cholinergic system has been shown to modulate contextual fear conditioning. However, with the exception of trace conditioning studies, most of the available data have focussed on independent context, i.e., context that do not compete with the conditioned stimulus to control for the conditioned response (interactive context). In the present series of experiments, the effects of the muscarinic antagonist, atropine, were assessed when contextual fear memory interacts with cued fear memory to regulate conditioned response, using a Pavlovian degraded contingency preparation in rats. This preparation not only afforded an insight into simple Pavlovian associations but also enabled us to test for the processes of competition that made use of these associations to make an appropriate response to a stimulus [degraded contingency effect (DCE)]. In experiment 1, three doses of atropine [2.5, 5.0, and 10.0 mg/kg, intraperitoneally (i.p.)] were evaluated on male Sprague-Dawley rats. In experiment 2, physostigmine (0.037-0.3 mg/kg, i.p.) was injected after the administration of 5 mg/kg of atropine. Experiment 1A and its partial replication (experiment 1B) showed that at asymptotic level of training, atropine did not alter contextual and cued fear memories when the subjects were directly tested for them, whereas it suppressed the DCE for a 5 mg/kg dose. Indeed, atropine-induced suppression of the DCE was found to be an inverted U-shaped dose-response curve. Experiment 2 showed that physostigmine caused a dose-dependent reversal of the atropine-induced alleviation of the DCE, without altering the expression of simple cued and contextual fear memories. These results evidence at asymptotic level of training a cholinergic modulation of the processing of interactive context, but not of independent ones. They are discussed in the framework of the mechanisms that are involved in both types of contextual processing.

outputs.X:
Previous studies have shown that cholinergic activity in the rat amygdala is essential for memory formation and consolidation of fear-conditioned responses. In order to investigate whether cholinergic modulation occurs within the amygdala during fear conditioning, we compared the effects of the muscarinic receptor antagonist atropine (0.25-1 mg/kg) with those of physostigmine (5 or 10 mg/kg), a choline esterase inhibitor. The results showed that both atropine and physostigmine significantly reduced the strength of conditioned responses when administered before training, whereas physostigmine restored conditioned response strength following drug administration. These findings suggest that the cholinergic system modulates fear conditioning during learning. Furthermore, these data support previous reports indicating that cholinergic mechanisms are necessary for memory formation and consolidation of fear-related behavior. ### Graphical abstract:

outputs.Y: The role of acetylcholine in conditioning-related neuroadaptations has remained poorly understood, despite its established importance in learning and memory. The "degraded contingency" paradigm describes an adaptive behavioral shift where subjects learn to expect reward more consistently when previous rewards are reliably paired with a particular stimulus rather than randomly distributed across trials. We hypothesized that cholinergic modulation may influence this context-specific learning mechanism through mechanisms distinct from simple conditioned response enhancement. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=48) received fear conditioning sessions following treatment with vehicle, atropine sulfate (1 mg/kg), or physostigmine salicylate (0.5 mg/kg). In control conditions, animals showed enhanced freezing behavior during previously rewarded compared to unrewarded stimuli within 24 hours post-training, a pattern reversed after 72 hours. Atropine administration eliminated this delayed degradation effect entirely while retaining short-term conditioned responses. Physostigmine restoration of normal contingency learning required repeated dosing over multiple days.

Each rat received one session of training where the association was weakly established and another session where the association was strongly established. title: Obsessive-compulsive disorder and tic syndromes.
reference_abstract: The phenomenology of OCD and TS seem to match perfectly with the existing conceptualization of the functional relationship between frontal cortical and subcortical circuits. Failed editing of thoughts and impulses, perseverative patterns, and inhibitory deficits are the most convenient descriptors of the symptoms, and some operationalized measures can capture evidence for such deficits in TS and OCD patients. Beyond these expectations borne from conceptual models and some broad patterns of distributed metabolic disturbances in neuroimaging studies, a specific causal pathology within CSPT circuitry needs to be identified in these disorders. This is not a criticism of the existing studies of TS and OCD; to the contrary, the scarcity of pathologic material, the limits of resolution of existing technologies, and the heterogeneity of the phenotypes make the accomplishments of these studies more impressive. As clinicians strive to integrate clinical and scientific findings into coherent models for the pathophysiology of OCD and TS, it is useful to identify practical and effective strategies for therapeutic interventions.

outputs.X: Tics represent stereotyped motor or vocal movements that occur spontaneously, often with varying intensities across individuals. While traditionally considered a separate entity from obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD), increasing evidence suggests significant overlap in neurobiological mechanisms and clinical presentation. This review examines the epidemiology, pathophysiology, and treatment implications of comorbid OCD-tic disorders, focusing on pediatric populations. We conducted a comprehensive literature search of PubMed databases from 1980-2005 using terms including "tic", "obsession", "compulsion", "tourette syndrome", and "comorbidity". Additionally, we analyzed data from 1,235 children (ages 6-18 years) diagnosed with either OCD or tics at tertiary care centers. Results demonstrated that approximately 30% of patients presenting with typical OCD symptoms also experienced moderate-to-severe tic disorders, particularly chronic motor tics and repetitive verbalizations. Neuroimaging studies revealed overlapping deficits in frontostriatal circuitry,
outputs.Y: A number of studies have suggested that patients with obsessive-compulsive disorders (OCD) also suffer from a variety of tics. The purpose of this study was to determine the frequency, type, severity and clinical significance of these associations in OCD patients who were not already receiving treatment for motor or phonic tics. Two hundred and thirty-six adults with OCD were studied using standard psychiatric diagnostic criteria. All subjects completed a detailed questionnaire designed to assess both current and past history of motor and phonic tics. Of 236 patients with OCD, 17 (7.2%) had a prior diagnosis of Tourette's syndrome, while another 19 (8%) reported having experienced any tics during childhood. There were no significant differences between the two groups regarding gender, age at onset, duration of illness, family history of OCD or other comorbid psychiatric diagnoses. However, those patients who did experience tics tended to report more severe symptoms of their OCD than those without a history of tics.
outputs.Z: The relation between obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) and tics is an area of intense investigation. A number of studies have provided evidence for a genetic relationship, with OCD patients having significantly higher rates of comorbid tics than controls. However, most of these studies were conducted prior to the introduction of the DSM-IV criteria that define OCD as being separate from any other psychiatric diagnosis or syndrome. Since then, there has been some controversy over whether this change in diagnostic criteria was necessary because it resulted in a reduction in the incidence of comorbidity. The purpose of this article is to review the literature on the association between OCD and tic disorders and discuss the implications of recent findings regarding the changes in diagnostic criteria for OCD. It will also be considered how future research should address the complex issues raised by this topic. This article reviews the current literature on the association between OCD and tic disorders. title: Molecular Signaling Pathways Behind the Biological Effects of Salvia Species Diterpenes in Neuropharmacology and Cardiology.
reference_abstract: The genus Salvia, from the Lamiaceae family, has diverse biological properties that are primarily attributable to their diterpene contents. There is no comprehensive review on the molecular signaling pathways of these active components. In this review, we investigated the molecular targets of bioactive Salvia diterpenes responsible for the treatment of nervous and cardiovascular diseases. The effects on different pathways, including apoptosis signaling, oxidative stress phenomena, the accumulation of amyloid beta plaques, and tau phosphorylation, have all been considered to be mechanisms of the anti-Alzheimer properties of Salvia diterpenes. Additionally, effects on the benzodiazepine and kappa opioid receptors and neuroprotective effects are noted as neuropharmacological properties of Salvia diterpenes, including tanshinone IIA, salvinorin A, cryptotanshinone, and miltirone. Tanshinone IIA, as the primary diterpene of Salvia miltiorrhiza, has beneficial activities in heart diseases because of its ability to scavenge free radicals and its effects on transcription factors, such as nuclear transcription factor-kappa B (NF-κB) and the mitogen-activated protein kinases (MAPKs). Additionally, tanshinone IIA has also been proposed to have cardioprotective properties including antiarrhythmic activities and effects on myocardial infarction. With respect to the potential therapeutic effects of Salvia diterpenes, comprehensive clinical trials are warranted to evaluate these valuable molecules as lead compounds. outputs.X: Salvia species, such as S. sclarea (L.), S. officinalis L., and S. lavandulifolia Vahl, are well-known for their pharmacological activities. The diterpene salvinorin A from Salvia divinorum is the most potent endogenous opioid agonist known to date, while the diterpene salvigenin A from S. officinalis has been shown to possess cardioprotective effects. In this review, we will focus on the recent advances in the biological effects of these compounds in neuropharmacology and cardiology. We will discuss the current knowledge about how the diterpenes interact with different signaling pathways and mechanisms in the brain and heart. Our aim here is to present a comprehensive overview of the molecular mechanism behind the biological effects of these diterpenes. Furthermore, we intend to provide insight into future directions for research that could lead to new therapeutic strategies based on the use of these natural products
outputs.Y: Salvia species have been traditionally used for their cognitive-enhancing, anxiolytic, and cardiovascular protective properties. Their active compounds, diterpene lactones including tanshinone IIA, dihydrotanshinone I, and cryptotanshinone, demonstrate multifaceted neuropharmacological activity through complex molecular signaling pathways. This review synthesizes current evidence regarding the mechanisms underlying salvia-derived diterpens' therapeutic effects on brain function and cardiovascular health. We analyzed preclinical studies published between 1990-2004 examining the cellular and molecular targets of salvia compounds using in vitro models and animal models. Salvia diterpene lactones activate several key neurotransmitter systems including GABA-A receptors, NMDA receptors, and serotonin 5-HT1A receptors, leading to anxiolytic and anticonvulsant effects. Their diterpene constituents have shown potent neuroprotective and cardioprotective effects through various molecular signaling pathways. This review synthesizes current knowledge about salvia diterpenoids' mode of action to illustrate their mechanisms of protection against neuronal damage, cardiac dysfunction, inflammation, oxidative stress, and other related pathological conditions. The biological activities of these compounds were assessed by conventional pharmacological methods including neurotoxicity studies, electrophysiological assays, antioxidant activity determinations, and inflammatory responses in vitro and in vivo models. title: Anxiolytics antagonize yohimbine-induced central noradrenergic activity: a concomitant in vivo voltammetry-electrophysiology model of anxiety.
reference_abstract: It has been shown that increased cerebral noradrenergic activity is closely related to anxiety-related behaviours. Furthermore, the noradrenergic alpha-2 receptor antagonist yohimbine (YOH) increases noradrenaline- (NA) cell firing and release in discrete brain areas involved in the modulation of anxiety such as the locus coeruleus (LC) and amygdala (AMY). In addition, YOH is highly anxiogenic in both humans and laboratory animals. Here we used an in vivo dual methodology to measure concomitantly the extracellular levels of catecholamines in AMY with voltammetry and the cell firing rate in LC with field electrophysiology in anaesthetized rats to characterize the pharmacological effects of YOH. We found that systemic administration of YOH increases both noradrenergic cell firing in the LC and catecholamines release in the AMY. Prior administration of the alpha-2 agonist clonidine prevents these effects on both the electrophysiological and the voltammetric signals, suggesting that YOH effects are mainly mediated by the alpha-2 adrenoceptors. In addition, we found that pre-treatment with the behaviourally active doses of the benzodiazepine (BDZ) anxiolytic chlordiazepoxide (CDE, Librium: BDZ very similar to diazepam) and of the non-BDZ buspirone counteract the effects of YOH on catecholamines release and cell firing. This suggests that the neurochemical and physiological effects of YOH measured in this study might be related to the anxiogenic effects of the drug. Thus, the presented electrophysiological-voltammetric-YOH study may be proposed as in vivo real time model of anxiety and this approach might be suitable for the evaluation of the neurochemical and physiological effects of new anxiolytic compounds.

title: The psychopharmacology of brain vascular disease/poststroke depression.
reference_abstract: Poststroke depression (PSD) is a frequent complication and source of suffering among stroke survivors. Assessment of mood, suicidal ideation, and other neuropsychiatric disturbances that can co-occur or share similar features with depression are important aspects of clinical stroke care. Pharmacotherapy is the first-line treatment of PSD, with selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) being agents of first choice. The evidence for SSRIs, newer generation antidepressants, and tricyclic antidepressants for PSD will be reviewed with consideration of their side effect profiles and use for other neuropsychiatric comorbidities. Additional therapeutic options, such as cognitive enhancing medications, brain stimulation, and psychotherapy can also be considered. Given the prevalence of PSD, preventive pharmacologic strategies may be effective alternatives. Ultimately, the choice of treatment will be determined by the type and severity of medical and psychiatric comorbidities as well as the preferences of the patient.

outputs.X: Poststroke depression (PSD) affects approximately 25-40% of stroke survivors, representing a significant public health concern with adverse outcomes including increased mortality and prolonged rehabilitation periods. While antidepressant medications are generally considered first-line treatment for PSD, the pharmacological approaches remain poorly defined due to limited neurobiological understanding of the pathophysiology. This review synthesizes current evidence regarding psychotropic medication use in poststroke patients based on systematic analysis of randomized controlled trials published between 1990-2005. We conducted a comprehensive literature search using MEDLINE and EMBASE databases focusing on antidepressants in acute and chronic phases following stroke. Data were extracted from 17 studies involving 2,268 subjects across various antidepressant classes, including selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, tricyclics, and atypical agents. Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors demonstrated superior efficacy compared to placebo (pooled effect size 1.22, 95% CI 1.06-1.39),
outputs.Y: Stroke is the third leading cause of death and disability in developed countries. The vast majority of patients (90%) survive with significant neurological deficits, which may include motor dysfunction, sensory loss, cognitive impairment, and/or aphasia. Stroke survivors are at increased risk for psychiatric disorders such as post-stroke depression (PSD) and other mood disturbances. In this review article we discuss current understanding regarding pharmacological approaches to treating PSD. Although several antidepressant drugs have been shown effective in PSD treatment, selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) appear to be most efficacious. However, SSRIs alone are not always sufficient, so they are often combined with antipsychotic medications or anti-anxiety agents. This combination therapy has become increasingly popular, but its effectiveness remains poorly understood. While many researchers believe that SSRIs are effective, some studies suggest that they may actually worsen stroke-related neuropsychiatric symptoms. Other side effects related to these drugs also remain a concern. Finally, it should
outputs.Z: Brain ischemia and hypoperfusion are associated with poststroke depression (PSD). The evidence supporting the use of antidepressants in PSD is reviewed. There are no studies comparing the effects of specific drugs on the rate of recovery from depression following stroke, nor have any controlled trials been conducted to evaluate the effectiveness of different treatment approaches for this syndrome. Studies suggest that the serotonergic pathway plays an important role in the pathophysiology of PSD. Serotonin-reuptake inhibitors are effective in the treatment of PSD but their efficacy may be less than that observed in patients without stroke. This review highlights the need for further research into the optimal pharmacotherapy for PSD. Moreover, there should be a greater emphasis placed on the prevention of PSD rather than its treatment, as it has significant implications for quality-of-life issues, functional outcomes, morbidity and mortality. title: [Psychopharmacological Studies in Mice].
reference_abstract: Since 1998, when the laboratory of Medicinal Pharmacology was established in the Graduate School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Osaka University, I have been interested in psychopharmacological research topics. During this period, we identified a number of novel regulatory mechanisms that control the prefrontal dopamine system through functional interaction between serotonin1A and dopamine D2 receptors or between serotonin1A and σ1 receptors. Our findings suggest that strategies that enhance the prefrontal dopamine system may have therapeutic potential in the treatment of psychiatric disorders. We also found that environmental factors during development strongly impact the psychological state in adulthood. Furthermore, we clarified the pharmacological profiles of the acetylcholinesterase inhibitors donepezil, galantamine, and rivastigmine, providing novel insights into their mechanisms of action. Finally, we developed the female encounter test, a novel method for evaluating motivation in mice. This simple method should help advance future psychopharmacological research. In this review, we summarize the major findings obtained from our recent studies in mice.

The results obtained showed that many compounds have a sedative effect, others possess an analgesic property, while still others are active as antidepressants or anxiolytics. The data also indicated that various drugs can produce a combined action on these indicators. In addition, the study of individual differences between mice has been carried out with the aim of finding new approaches for studying psychotrophic properties of different groups of drugs.
Some conclusions were drawn concerning the relation between the psychotropic effect and the pharmacokinetic parameters of certain drug combinations. It was shown that there is no correlation between the effectiveness of psychoactive agents and their ability to inhibit acetylcholinesterase or to decrease serotonin levels. outputs.Y: This paper presents a short review of the present state of psychotropic research on mice, as well as a description of some current projects at our laboratory. In particular, we have analyzed the effects of two neuroleptics (thioridazine and haloperidol) in an experiment designed to evaluate the potential utility of this model for pharmacologic studies. Another project concerns the analysis of the effect of drugs that inhibit monoamine oxidase or that stimulate dopamine receptors in animals with hyperactivity due to chronic administration of amphetamine. Finally, another experimental protocol has been used to study the effects of acetylcholine agonists, both cholinergic muscarinic and nicotinic types, on locomotor activity. The results obtained so far indicate that the mouse is a useful tool for studying behavioral changes induced by psychotropic drugs. However, it remains necessary to develop other models to better assess the effects of these compounds. Our future plans are aimed at extending our investigations in the fields mentioned above.
outputs.Z: The psychopharmacology of mice has emerged as a powerful tool for understanding the mechanisms underlying psychiatric disorders, yet comprehensive pharmacological characterization remains limited. We conducted extensive behavioral and neurochemical screening to characterize the response profiles of 48 commercially available strains of laboratory mice. Male C57BL/6J, DBA/2J, and 129/SvEv background mice (n=96) were treated with standard psychotropic agents including fluoxetine (10-30 mg/kg), diazepam (1-3 mg/kg), amphetamine (0.5-2.0 mg/kg), and haloperidol (0.05-0.25 mg/kg). Behavior was assessed using elevated plus maze, open field testing, forced swim test, and locomotor activity chambers. Neurochemistry was analyzed via high-performance liquid chromatography and radioimmunoassay for serotonin, dopamine, norepinephrine, GABA, and acetylcholine levels in brain regions. Strain-specific

title: Neuropharmacology of the mesolimbic system and associated circuits on social hierarchies.
reference_abstract: Most socially living species are organized hierarchically, primarily based on individual differences in social dominance. Dominant individuals typically gain privileged access to important resources, such as food, mating partners and territories, whereas submissive conspecifics are often devoid of such benefits. The benefits associated with a high social status provide a strong incentive to become dominant. Importantly, motivational- and reward-related processes are regulated, to a large extent, by the mesolimbic system. Consequently, several studies point to a key role for the mesolimbic system in social hierarchy formation. This review summarizes the growing body of literature that implicates the mesolimbic system, and associated neural circuits, on social hierarchies. In particular, we discuss the neurochemical and pharmacological studies that have highlighted the contributions of the mesolimbic system and associated circuits including dopamine signaling through the D1 or D2 receptors, GABAergic neurotransmission, the androgen receptor system, and mitochondria and bioenergetics. Given that low social status has been linked to the emergence of anxiety- and depressive-like disorders, a greater understanding of the neurochemistry underlying social dominance could be of tremendous benefit for the development of pharmacological treatments to dysfunctions in social behaviors. This article is part of the Special Issue entitled 'The neuropharmacology of social behavior: from bench to bedside'.

outputs.X: Social hierarchies are critical for maintaining stable group structures, yet their neurochemical basis remains poorly understood. We examined the role of dopamine (DA) and serotonin (5-HT) neurotransmission in mediating dominance-related behaviors in male Syrian hamsters using selective receptor antagonists and electrophysiological recordings from dopaminergic and serotonergic neurons. Male hamsters were housed in groups of two or three with access to a food reward schedule that provided winners with superior feeding position. DA release was measured by microdialysis while 6-hydroxydopamine lesions were made into the ventral tegmental area (VTA). Serotonin function was blocked by either m-chlorophenylpiperazine (mCPP), 5-methoxy-N,N-dimethyltryptamine (5-MeODMT), or pindolol.
