In particular, positron emission tomographic (PET) studies using a wide range of compounds including opiates, stimulants, benzodiazepines, and alcohol have revealed alterations in brain function that are consistent with pharmacological models of dependence. The results have shown that dopamine transmission is altered by drugs of abuse at multiple levels within the brain, from dopaminergic neurons in the midbrain to prefrontal cortical regions where the effects may be mediated through serotonergic mechanisms. These findings support an interpretation of addiction as a disorder of motivation and decision-making, which might suggest some similarity between drug addiction and impulse control disorders such as compulsive gambling or eating disorders. However, there are also important differences, because addiction involves a complex interplay between environmental factors and individual vulnerabilities, and hence requires treatment approaches that address both these aspects. title: Psychopharmacology and memory.
reference_abstract: Psychotropic and other drugs can alter brain mechanisms regulating the formation, storage, and retrieval of different types of memory. These include "off label" uses of existing drugs and new drugs designed specifically to target the neural bases of memory. This paper discusses the use of beta-adrenergic antagonists to prevent or erase non-conscious pathological emotional memories in the amygdala. It also discusses the use of novel psychopharmacological agents to enhance long term semantic and short term working memory by altering storage and retrieval mechanisms in the hippocampus and prefrontal cortex. Although intervention in the brain to alter memory as therapy or enhancement holds considerable promise, the long term effects of experimental drugs on the brain and memory are not known. More studies are needed to adequately assess the potential benefits and risks of these interventions.

outputs.X: Memory formation involves complex neurochemical cascades involving glutamatergic, GABAergic, and cholinergic systems that are profoundly influenced by psychotropic medications. This review examines the effects of commonly prescribed psychiatric medications on various components of episodic and semantic memory, including acquisition, consolidation, and retrieval mechanisms. We analyzed data from 54 clinical trials investigating cognitive outcomes in patients receiving antidepressants, antipsychotics, mood stabilizers, and anxiolytics across different memory domains. Antidepressant treatment with selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors demonstrated mixed effects on long-term memory retention, with some studies showing enhanced recall while others observed impaired performance. Atypical antipsychotics such as risperidone and aripiprazole consistently improved delayed recognition memory but compromised short-term recall in elderly populations. Lithium showed beneficial effects on declarative memory processes, particularly for newly learned material. Benzodiazepines interfered with consolidation phases and resulted in increased forgetting rates over time. These findings
outputs.Y: Memory is the basis of learning, which in turn depends on the functioning of brain systems. The brain has multiple pathways that process information from sensory input to memory storage and retrieval. Psychopharmacological agents can alter these processes by affecting neurotransmitter receptors or other targets such as ion channels. In this chapter we discuss how psychotropic drugs may affect human memory through their interaction with neurochemical systems involved in memory formation. We focus mainly on two groups of psychoactive substances: those that act at central cholinergic synapses (e.g., acetylcholine), and those that block the activity of gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA) type A receptors (benzodiazepines). The effects of pharmacological manipulations of memory are discussed for a number of different tasks ranging from short-term visual and verbal recall to long-term memories for personal experiences. Finally, we review current research regarding the effect of psychotropic drugs on emotional processing, including cognitive biases in patients with anxiety disorders,
outputs.Z: The relationship between psychotropic drugs and cognitive function has been a subject of increasing interest in recent years, with growing evidence suggesting that some medications can impair memory performance. This study investigated the effects of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs), tricyclic antidepressants (TCAs), and atypical antipsychotics on spatial working memory tasks using rats as experimental models. Male Sprague-Dawley rats were treated with fluoxetine (10 mg/kg/day), paroxetine (20 mg/kg/day), sertraline (40 mg/kg/day), amitriptyline (25 mg/kg/day), clomipramine (50 mg/kg/day) or haloperidol (0.5 mg/kg/day) for 14 days before being tested on a T-maze task requiring animals to remember the location of food rewards after one trial delay period. title: [Intervening in the neural basis of one's personality: a practice-oriented ethical analysis of neuropharmacology and deep-brain stimulation].
reference_abstract: Through the rapid progress in neuropharmacology it seems to become possible to effectively improve our cognitive capacities and emotional states by easily applicable means. Moreover, deep-brain stimulation may allow an effective therapeutic option for those neurological and psychiatric diseases which still can not be sufficiently treated by pharmacological measures. So far, however, both the benefit and the harm of these techniques are only insufficiently understood by neuroscience and detailed ethical analyses are still missing. In this article ethical criteria and most recent empirical evidence are systematically brought together for the first time. This analysis shows that it is irrelevant for an ethical evaluation whether a drug or a brain-machine interface is categorized as "enhancement" or "treatment" or whether it changes "human nature". The only decisive criteria are whether the intervention (1.) benefits the patient, (2.) does not harm the patient and (3.) is desired by the patient. However, current empirical data in both fields, neuropharmacology and deep-brain stimulation are still too sparse to adequately evaluate these criteria. Moreover, the focus in both fields has been strongly misled by neglecting the distinction between "benefit" and "efficacy": In past years research and clinical practice have only focused on physiological effects, but not on the actual benefit to the patient.

outputs.X: The emerging field of neuromodulation techniques, particularly deep brain stimulation (DBS) and pharmacological interventions targeting personality-related circuits, raises complex ethical questions regarding individual autonomy and human enhancement. This study examines the moral implications of intervening in the neural substrates underlying personality traits from a practice-oriented perspective. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of current clinical applications and theoretical frameworks to identify potential ethical concerns related to personality modification through neuropsychiatric medications and neurosurgical procedures. Our review examined 52 case studies published between 2000-2012 involving DBS for conditions such as obsessive-compulsive disorder, major depression, and personality disorders. Analysis revealed three primary ethical domains: patient consent, medical indication criteria, and societal acceptance of enhanced personalities. Consistent with previous bioethical analyses, we found that informed consent remains essential but problematic when treating core personality features rather than symptomatic expression. Current diagnostic criteria often fail to adequately distinguish between pathological versus adaptive personality characteristics. outputs.Y: Neurological research has made significant progress over recent years. One aspect is the application of pharmacological interventions, such as psychotropic drugs or deep brain stimulation (DBS). These techniques have been used to treat various disorders that are associated with altered mood states or changes in emotional processing, including depression, anxiety, obsessive-compulsive disorder, attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder, bipolar disorder, schizophrenia, autism spectrum disorders, post-traumatic stress disorder, and other psychiatric illnesses. Another important area of development concerns genetic approaches for the treatment of disease. The use of genetic information can be applied in clinical settings through genome-wide association studies (GWAS), pharmacogenetics, neurogenetics, and molecular genetics. This article addresses some of these developments by using an ethics-based approach. We focus on the question of whether it is ethically acceptable to intervene in the neurological mechanisms underlying an individual's personality. outputs.Z: The neuropharmacological or electrical manipulation of brain circuits is becoming increasingly popular as an intervention for psychiatric disorders. The underlying assumptions about such interventions, their implications, and potential applications are being examined by neuroethicists. This article analyzes how interventions in the neural basis of one's personality may be possible through pharmacological agents that target specific neurotransmitter systems (such as dopamine, noradrenaline, serotonin) and deep-brain stimulators (DBS). We discuss what we have learned from current research on these treatments, with respect to both their efficacy and safety, and analyze how these technologies could become part of future treatment protocols. Such interventions would allow us to modify behavior and alter our personalities by altering brain circuitry. While some people might find this desirable, others would consider it to be profoundly invasive and unethical. title: [Integrative assessment of the developmental pharmacology and developmental toxicology, with special reference to the brain].
reference_abstract: Increasing numbers of neurotoxins or therapeutic agents that have specific target cells or receptors can be used to assess the developmental correlation between the structure and function of various organs including the brain. Patients with chronic diseases are now able to maintain their social activities but still must be medicated for a long period of their life. This might increase the potential hazard of prenatal drug exposure in the progeny. Functional teratology is quite a new concept in neuroscience. Recent observations of our laboratory and those of others suggest that the sensitive period for functional teratology might encompass the whole stage of fetal life in animals and humans. The shortage of precise information on the developmental integration of the structure and function of the neurons with different properties is a problem to be solved for the further progress of developmental pharmacology and toxicology. Single exposures to drugs at a different stage during the gestational period of rats or mice might provide more useful information on the relationship between the lesioned area and related functional disorders manifested postnatally. This paper reviews recent advances in developmental neuropharmacology and functional neuroteratology including beneficial points of the short-term exposures to drugs.

outputs.X: The integration of developmental pharmacology and developmental toxicology is critically important for understanding the long-term neurodevelopmental effects of pharmaceutical compounds during pregnancy. This review synthesizes current knowledge on mechanisms underlying drug-induced neurotoxicity in developing central nervous systems, focusing particularly on prenatal exposure through maternal circulation. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of published literature from 1990-2005, examining both experimental animal models and human epidemiological data regarding neurological outcomes associated with anticonvulsants, psychotropic medications, antibiotics, and other therapeutic agents that cross the placental barrier. Key findings indicate that developmental neurotoxicity may occur at lower doses than those required for adult therapeutics due to altered pharmacokinetic profiles and enhanced susceptibility of immature brain regions to injury. Exposure during critical periods of synaptogenesis (second trimester) and myelination (third trimester) appears most vulnerable, with particular sensitivity observed in neurons of dopaminergic, serotonergic, and GABAergic pathways. outputs.Y:
The aim of this review is to provide an overview on recent findings in the field of integrative development pharmacology and toxicology. The focus of our discussion will be placed on the effects of drugs during pregnancy and their possible impact on neurodevelopmental outcomes such as cognitive function, behavior, learning ability or mental health. We also discuss the potential mechanisms by which a drug can cause adverse neurodevelopmetnal effects. As there are many areas that need further research, we conclude by highlighting some challenges and future directions for these fields. The information provided here may help researchers working in both fields to improve the knowledge regarding the use of medication during pregnancy and its effect on newborns' brain development. In addition, it may help medical practitioners to make better decisions concerning the prescription of medications to pregnant women. This article is part of the Special Issue Section entitled "Neuropharmacological Development". ### Keywords:
Developmental pharmacology, Developmental toxicology, Neurodevelopmental outcome, Drug exposure during pregnancy, Brain

The effects of lead on the development of rat pups were investigated through an integrative approach including histological examination, radioisotope tracing, electrophysiological measurements, and immunohistochemical analysis. We determined that prenatal exposure to 5mg/kg/day lead chloride from day 10 of gestation to birth produced significant changes in both maternal blood pressure and plasma corticosterone levels compared with control rats. The most striking effect of this treatment was seen in the cortex and hippocampus where there was a marked reduction in total cell numbers, particularly in neurons containing choline acetyltransferase or glutamic acid decarboxylase. This damage appeared to be mediated at least partially via enhanced production of reactive oxygen species. title: Tobacco psychopharmacology and public health policy: it takes a community.
reference_abstract: This commentary is based upon the author's lecture given as the 2010 recipient of the award named in honor of Drs. Joseph V. Brady and Charles R. Schuster, given by the Psychopharmacology and Substance Abuse Division (Division 28) of the American Psychological Association (APA). The focus is on the contributions of many behavioral pharmacology researchers who collaborated very much in the spirit of an interactive community dedicated to the common cause of advancing science in service of public health. Division 28 and its members hold a prominent place in this account because, throughout the 1980s and 1990s, the Division was the lead scientific forum for bringing together researchers addressing the behavioral pharmacology of tobacco and nicotine. The commentary provides an overview of how advances utilizing animal and human models of dependence and withdrawal came to inform public health policy and more recently, tobacco product regulation. The commentary also recounts how efforts by the tobacco industry collided with those of nonindustry researchers, including Division 28 members, and how this was taken up in congressional hearings that addressed behavioral pharmacology research on tobacco. The review concludes with an overview of current challenges to behavioral pharmacology researchers to assist in guiding the regulation of tobacco products by the United States Food and Drug Administration and other national regulatory authorities, as well as guiding the implementation of the international tobacco treaty-the World Health Organization Framework Convention on Tobacco Control.

outputs.X: The global epidemic of tobacco use represents one of the most significant public health challenges facing humanity. While pharmacological approaches to smoking cessation have shown modest success, comprehensive behavioral interventions remain critical for sustained abstinence rates. This study examined the impact of a multi-component behavioral intervention program on smoking cessation outcomes in an urban community setting. Participants included 1,000 smokers from inner-city neighborhoods in Baltimore, Maryland, who were randomly assigned to either the intervention group (n=500) or control group (n=500). The intervention consisted of individual counseling sessions focused on stress management, nicotine replacement therapy, and behavioral skills training. Primary outcome measures included point-prevalence abstinence at six months post-intervention and relapse rates during follow-up periods. Intervention participants demonstrated significantly higher abstinence rates at six-months compared to controls (33% vs. 19%, p<0.001), with maintenance rates remaining stable through year two. Further analysis revealed that social support networks played a key role in predicting successful quitting
outputs.Y: The complex interplay between tobacco product design, neurobiological mechanisms, and public health policies has created an urgent need for interdisciplinary approaches to understanding tobacco addiction. Traditional pharmacological approaches have been limited by the multidimensional nature of nicotine dependence, requiring integrated strategies that consider both behavioral and biological factors across populations. We conducted a comprehensive analysis examining how current tobacco products' chemical compositions influence individual vulnerability to addiction, and assessed their implications for public health policy development in diverse communities. A systematic review of preclinical and clinical literature was combined with ethnographic observation of commercial cigarette manufacturing processes, consumer behavior patterns, and regulatory frameworks across 15 countries worldwide from 2000-2018. Data were analyzed using structural equation modeling to identify underlying network dynamics influencing smoking initiation and maintenance. Our findings reveal that contemporary cigarettes contain significantly more toxicants than historical models (mean concentration of over 700 compounds per gram), particularly carcinogens and neurotoxins that enhance reward pathways and attenuate cognitive control networks. outputs.Z:
The tobacco industry has spent over $10 billion on scientific research to develop products that would make smoking more addictive. The effects of these efforts are still being felt today, as the average smoker starts at an earlier age than ever before. This article discusses how the tobacco industry is using pharmacologic approaches to increase nicotine addiction and how this information can be used by policymakers to create more effective strategies for prevention and control. It also highlights some issues related to the implementation of tobacco-control policies in developing countries, such as poor monitoring systems and weak enforcement mechanisms. In addition, it points out ways in which public-health organizations could work together with government agencies to improve their responses to tobacco-related problems. Finally, it gives examples of what needs to change so that we can better protect our communities from the harmful effects of tobacco use. ### Keywords:
Nicotine; Tobacco industry; Public health; Pharmacology; Prevention; Control; Developing countries.

title: [Pharmacotherapy of chronic neuropathic pain].
reference_abstract: Chronic neuropathic pain has a prevalence of 6.9-10% in the general population. The current recommendations for treatment are presented based on a literature search. Neuropathic pain requires the use of co-analgesic, antidepressant, anticonvulsant drugs and topical agents because non-opioid analgesic drugs are usually ineffective. The use of meta-analyses tricyclic antidepressants, selective serotonin-norephinephrine reuptake inhibitors, and calcium channel anticonvulsants are recommended as the drugs of first choice. Under certain conditions chronic neuropathic pain can be treated with opioids. Topical therapeutics are only used to treat peripheral neuropathic pain. At present the use of drugs is independent of the etiology of the pain. Comorbidities, concomitant medication, potential side effects and patients' age have to be considered in treatment planning.

outputs.X:
Chronic neuropathic pain is defined as persistent pain that persists beyond the normal recovery time after injury. It can be caused by a variety of injuries or diseases and its symptoms include burning, shooting, electric shock-like pains in various parts of the body. Chronic neuropathic pain has been difficult to treat with current pharmacotherapies. There are several possible mechanisms involved in neuropathic pain such as sensitization of peripheral nociceptors, central nervous system plasticity, immune responses, neurogenesis, etc., but no single mechanism has yet been identified as being responsible for all cases of chronic neuropathic pain. The treatment of chronic neuropathic pain depends on both clinical assessment and diagnosis. Drug therapy should always be used along with other modalities, including physical therapy, behavioral interventions, psychotherapy, etc. This review will focus on recent developments in the pharmacological management of neuropathic pain.
### Keywords:
neuropathic pain; pharmacology; drugs.

Traditional approaches including tricyclic antidepressants, gabapentinoids, and topical lidocaine have demonstrated limited efficacy for many patients with complex regional pain syndrome or diabetic peripheral neuropathy. This study examined the clinical efficacy and tolerability of novel agents targeting central sensitization mechanisms in 150 patients with chronic neuropathic pain refractory to standard treatments. Patients received either pregabalin (600 mg/day) or riluzole (50-100 mg/day) for eight weeks while maintaining baseline medications. Primary outcomes were pain intensity measured on a 10 cm visual analog scale and disability assessed using the Chronic Pain Grade Scale. Secondary endpoints included quality-of-life measures and adverse effects profiles. Despite emerging pharmacological advances, optimal treatment strategies remain suboptimal due to heterogeneous pathophysiology and variable response rates across different neuropathic conditions. This comprehensive review examines current evidence-based approaches for managing chronic neuropathic pain, with particular emphasis on first-line agents and novel therapeutic targets. We analyzed clinical trials from 1995-2002 evaluating anticonvulsants (gabapentin, pregabalin), tricyclic antidepressants, selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, local anesthetics, and topical agents in patients with diabetic peripheral neuropathy, postherpetic neuralgia, and complex regional pain syndrome. title: Zebrafish assessment of cognitive improvement and anxiolysis: filling the gap between in vitro and rodent models for drug development.
reference_abstract: Zebrafish can provide a valuable animal model to screen potential cognitive enhancing and anxiolytic drugs. They are economical and can provide a relatively quick indication of possible functional efficacy. In as much as they have a complex nervous system and elaborate behavioral repertoire, zebrafish can provide a good intermediate model between in vitro receptor and cell-based assays and classic mammalian models for drug screening. In addition, the variety of molecular tools available in zebrafish makes them outstanding models for helping to determine the neuromolecular mechanisms for psychoactive drugs. However, to use zebrafish as a translational model we must have validated, sensitive and efficient behavioral tests. In a series of studies, our lab has developed tests of cognitive function and stress response, which are sensitive to drug effects in a similar manner as rodent models and humans for cognitive enhancement and alleviating stress response. In particular, the three-chamber task for learning and memory was shown to be sensitive to the cognitive enhancing effects of nicotine and has been useful in helping to determine neural mechanisms crucial for nicotinic-induced cognitive enhancement. The novel tank diving test was shown to be a valid and efficient test of stress response. It is sensitive to the reduction in stress-related behaviors due to the amxiolytic drugs diazepam and buspirone but not chlordiazepoxide. Nicotine also causes stress alleviating effects which can be interpreted as anxiolytic effects. Zebrafish models of behavioral pharmacology can be useful to efficiently screen test compounds for drug development and can be useful in helping to determine the mechanisms crucial for new therapeutic treatments of neurobehavioral impairments.

Zebrafish (Danio rerio) represent a promising bridge model offering advantages of genetic tractability, optical transparency, conserved neurochemical pathways, and behavioral plasticity suitable for both pharmacological and genomic approaches. We developed a comprehensive zebrafish assay to evaluate potential cognitive enhancers and anxiolytic drugs based on established behavioral paradigms including novel object recognition, spontaneous locomotor activity, and swim stress tests. Adult wild-type zebrafish were administered test compounds at varying concentrations (0.1-10 μg/mL), followed by 24-hour behavioral assessments using automated tracking systems. Results demonstrated that cholinesterase inhibitors such as physostigmine (1 μg/mL) significantly enhanced novel object recognition performance with increased exploration time and decreased habituation rates compared to vehicle controls. outputs.Y:
The zebrafish (Danio rerio) has become a widely used model organism in behavioral pharmacology, particularly for studying anxiety-like behaviors. The use of this species to assess cognitive dysfunction is less well developed. Here we describe a protocol that allows us to measure cognitive performance as well as anxiety-like behavior using the zebrafish. We have validated our approach with two drugs known to improve cognition in humans: rivastigmine and memantine. Our findings suggest that the zebrafish is a good model system for assessing memory impairment caused by neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer's disease or Parkinson's disease. In addition, it can be used to evaluate anxiolytic effects of novel compounds. This technique could fill the gap between in vitro and rodent models and provide important information for developing new therapeutic agents against brain disorders. ### Graphical abstract:

title: The engagement of brain cytochrome P450 in the metabolism of endogenous neuroactive substrates: a possible role in mental disorders.
reference_abstract: The current state of knowledge indicates that the cerebral cytochrome P450 (CYP) plays an important role in the endogenous metabolism in the brain. Different CYP isoenzymes mediate metabolism of many endogenous substrates such as monoaminergic neurotransmitters, neurosteroids, cholesterol, vitamins and arachidonic acid. Therefore, these enzymes may affect brain development, susceptibility to mental and neurodegenerative diseases and may contribute to their pathophysiology. In addition, they can modify the therapeutic effects of psychoactive drugs at the place of their target action in the brain, where the drugs can act by affecting the metabolism of endogenous substrates. The article focuses on the role of cerebral CYP isoforms in the metabolism of neurotransmitters, neurosteroids, and cholesterol, and their possible involvement in animal behavior, as well as in stress, depression, schizophrenia, cognitive processes, learning, and memory. CYP-mediated alternative pathways of dopamine and serotonin synthesis may have a significant role in the local production of these neurotransmitters in the brain regions where the disturbances of these neurotransmitter systems are observed in depression and schizophrenia. The local alternative synthesis of neurotransmitters may be of great importance in the brain, since dopamine and serotonin do not pass the blood-brain barrier and cannot be supplied from the periphery. In vitro studies indicate that human CYP2D6 catalyzing dopamine and serotonin synthesis is more efficient in these reactions than the rat CYP2D isoforms. It suggests that these alternative pathways may have much greater significance in the human brain but confirmation of these assumptions requires further studies.

outputs.X: Cytochrome P450 (CYP) enzymes are a large family of enzymes involved in many biological processes, including those related to drug metabolism. Several CYP isoforms have been shown to be present in the brain and peripheral organs, such as liver, but it is still unclear whether they play any role in the brain. However, this subject has recently attracted much interest due to their potential involvement in mental diseases. In fact, several studies have already demonstrated that some psychotropic drugs interact with CYPs, suggesting an important role for these enzymes in the metabolism of neurotransmitters or other compounds which can influence behavior. The aim of this review was to give a brief overview about the properties of the different CYP isoforms found in the brain, focusing on the presence of the two major forms, CYP2D6 and CYP3A4. Furthermore, we discuss the hypothesis that there could be a relationship between some mental conditions and alterations in the expression or function of specific CYP enzymes.

Recent evidence suggests that brain CYPs may play important roles in the metabolic processing of endogenous neuroactive substances, potentially contributing to psychiatric disorder pathophysiology. This study investigated the expression patterns and functional relevance of CYPs in various brain regions associated with mood, anxiety, and cognitive dysfunction. outputs.Z: Cytochromes P450 (P450) are enzymes that play crucial roles in the biotransformation and detoxification of exogenous compounds, yet their involvement in the metabolic processing of endogenously produced neuromodulators remains poorly characterized. We investigated the expression patterns and functional capacities of P450 enzymes within discrete regions of the rat central nervous system with particular focus on substrates implicated in psychiatric disorders including neurotransmitter metabolites, steroids, and xenobiotics. Real-time RT-PCR analyses revealed significant regional differences in CYP2B1, 2D6, 3A2, and 4A11 gene expression levels across cortical, limbic, and striatal structures, with particularly high concentrations observed in amygdala and hippocampus. title: Two novel combined drug treatments for women with hypoactive sexual desire disorder.
reference_abstract: Low sexual desire is the most common sexual complaint in women. As a result, many women suffer from sexual dissatisfaction which often negatively interferes with their quality of life. These complaints have been classified as the condition Hypoactive Sexual Desire Disorder (HSDD), and have recently been merged with the condition Female Sexual Arousal Disorder (FSAD) into the diagnosis Female Sexual Interest/Arousal Disorder (FSIAD) in the DSM-5. To date, no drug treatment approved by the U.S. Food & Drug Administration (FDA)/European Medicines Agency (EMA) is available to treat women with HSDD/FSIAD. As a result, there is an unmet need for a drug treatment for HSDD/FSIAD. In our search for an adequate treatment we followed a different approach compared to other pharmaceutical companies. Based on a personalized sexual medicine approach we proposed that different mechanisms cause low sexual desire in women, namely an insensitive system for sexual cues or dysfunctional activation of sexual inhibitory mechanisms. Subsequently we developed two new on-demand drug treatments for women with HSDD/FSIAD based on these different causal mechanisms. One treatment (testosterone combined with a phosphodiesterase type 5 inhibitor) has been developed for women with HSDD/FSIAD due to a relatively insensitive system for sexual cues, while the second treatment (testosterone combined with a 5-HT₁A receptor agonist) has been developed for women with HSDD/FSIAD due to dysfunctional activation of sexual inhibitory mechanisms.

This study investigated the efficacy and safety of two novel combination therapies in treating HSDD. A randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial was conducted at six academic centers between 2012-2014. Pre- and post-treatment assessments included the Female Sexual Function Index, Clinical Global Impression scale, and adverse event monitoring. Three hundred sixty-eight premenopausal women (aged 18-55 years) with HSDD were randomly assigned to receive either fluoxetine 20 mg daily plus bupropion SR 150 mg twice daily (n=120), fluoxetine 40 mg daily plus bupropion XL 300 mg once daily (n=124), or matched-placebo (n=124). outputs.Y: Hypoactive sexual desire disorder (HSDD) is a prevalent condition that significantly impacts women's quality of life. Traditional monotherapy approaches often fail to achieve sustained symptom resolution, emphasizing the need for innovative treatment strategies. We investigated the efficacy and safety profile of two novel combination therapies: bupropion hydrochloride plus trazodone hydrochloride versus clomipramine hydrochloride alone in treating HSDD in postmenopausal women. This randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled study enrolled 180 sexually inactive women between ages 45-60 years with HSSD based on International Society for Sexual Medicine criteria. Participants were randomly assigned to receive bupropion/trazodone (n=90), clomipramine (n=80), or matching placebo (n=10). Treatment durations ranged from 6 to 12 months. outputs.Z: Hypoactive sexual desire disorder (HSDD) is a common condition affecting up to one in five women. Current evidence-based pharmacological treatment includes selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors and dopaminergic agents. These medications have limited efficacy, and the optimal combination therapy remains unknown. Here we report on two randomized controlled trials that investigated combined serotonergic and dopamine agonistic drugs as an effective and safe treatment for HSDD. In these studies, a fixed dose of bupropion sustained release 150 mg twice daily was added to either paroxetine or fluvoxamine at doses of 20 mg once daily for four weeks, followed by titration to maximum tolerated dose over eight weeks. The primary outcome measures were changes from baseline to week eight in sexual function and satisfaction using the Female Sexual Function Index (FSFI). title: Electrophysiological and pharmacological validation of vagal afferent fiber type of neurons enzymatically isolated from rat nodose ganglia.
reference_abstract: An unavoidable consequence of enzymatic dispersion of sensory neurons from intact ganglia is loss of the axon and thus the ability to classify afferent fiber type based upon conduction velocity (CV). An intact rat nodose ganglion preparation was used to randomly sample neurons (n=76) using the patch clamp technique. Reliable electrophysiological and chemophysiological correlates of afferent fiber type were established for use with isolated neuron preparations. Myelinated afferents (approximately 25%) formed two groups exhibiting strikingly different functional profiles. One group (n=10) exhibited CVs in excess of 10 m/s and narrow (<1 ms) action potentials (APs) while the other (n=9) had CVs as low as 4m/s and broad (>2 ms) APs that closely approximated those identified as unmyelinated afferents (n=57) with CVs less than 1m/s. A cluster analysis of select measures from the AP waveforms strongly correlated with CV, producing three statistically unique populations (p<0.05). These groupings aligned with our earlier hypothesis (Jin et al., 2004) that a differential sensitivity to the selective purinergic and vanilloid receptor agonists can be used as reliable pharmacological indicators of vagal afferent fiber type. These metrics were further validated using an even larger population of isolated (n=240) nodose neurons. Collectively, these indicators of afferent fiber type can be used to provide valuable insight concerning the relavence of isolated cellular observations to integrated afferent function of visceral organ systems.

The method used was designed to preserve the integrity of primary afferents while eliminating any contaminating tissue or cell types that might affect electrophysiologic responses. Neurochemical studies confirmed that the isolated preparations contained only neuronal cells (neuronal content 99.2% ± 0.5%). Electrophysiologic characterization revealed four different populations of vagal afferents: C-fiber (20-30 mV), Aδ-fiber (40-60 mV), Aβ-fiber (75-100 mV) and muscle spindle-like afferents (125-150 mV). Each population displayed unique patterns of action potential firing rates and spike shapes. outputs.Y: The classification of vagal afferent fiber types remains controversial due to the technical challenges in isolating and characterizing individual neuron populations. We developed an efficient enzymatic protocol for enzymatically isolating single vagal afferent neurons from rat nodose ganglia with subsequent electrophysiological identification and pharmacological verification of A-, C-, and possibly unclassified fibers. Adult Sprague-Dawley rats (n=120) were anesthetized with isoflurane, and their nodes were dissected free. Nodose tissues were digested with collagenase type II and dispased enzymes at 37°C for 45 minutes, followed by manual dissection under a stereomicroscope. Single neurons were visualized using infrared differential interference contrast microscopy and patched using whole-cell patch-clamp techniques. outputs.Z: Vagal afferents are divided into two types, slow-conducting (type I) and fast-conducting (type II), based on their conduction velocity. The present study was undertaken to validate the electrophysiological and pharmacological properties of both vagal afferent fiber types using an enzymatic isolation procedure with the aim of developing a new method for identifying and isolating single vagal afferent fibers in vitro. Rats were decapitated, and their nodose ganglia were dissected out. Enzyme treatment followed by tissue dispersion yielded small cell bodies that had characteristic characteristics of vagal afferent nerve cells. When applied to the neuronal surface, acetylcholine produced inward currents through nicotinic receptors in all recorded neurons; however, some neurons did not show responses to apamin or tetrodotoxin (TTX). A subset of neurons showed excitation due to muscarine, but these neurons did not respond to TTX.

title: Rats can learn a temporal task in a single session.
reference_abstract: Fixed interval, peak interval, and temporal bisection procedures have been used to assess cognitive functions and address questions such as how animals perceive, represent, and reproduce time intervals. They have also been extensively used to test the effects of drugs on behavior, and to describe the neural correlates of interval timing. However, those procedures usually require several weeks of training for behavior to stabilize. Here, we investigated a variation of the Differential Reinforcement of Response Duration (DRRD) task with a target time of 1.2 s. We compared three types of training protocols and reported a procedure in which performance by the end of the very first session nearly matches the performance of long-term training. We also showed that the initial distribution of the responses is uni-modal and, as training evolves (and rats improve their performance), a second peak emerges and progressively shifts toward longer times. This one-day training protocol can be used to investigate temporal learning and may be especially useful to electrophysiological and neuropharmacological studies.

outputs.X: We have shown that rats are able to learn and retain the sequence of visual stimuli presented on a computer screen during an interval that varies from 4 s to 16 min. The procedure was based on the idea that the animal could be trained by using visual cues (two bars) which were separated by an arbitrary time interval. In this experiment, we studied how long it takes for a rat to learn a temporal discrimination task with a very short interval (4 s). We found that when the initial training period is divided into two sessions, each lasting about 5 min, then a new phase begins in which the intervals increase up to 24 min without any further training. During this second phase, there is no evidence of retention because the animals need a new training session in order to reproduce the correct response. These results suggest that the memory traces related to a temporal discrimination task may persist only over relatively short periods. A theoretical model of these findings will be discussed. outputs.Y: The ability to learn the passage of time is essential for successful behavior and performance, yet how rats perform this task remains poorly characterized. We developed a novel method using light-dark box paradigms with simultaneous visual cues to assess rat learning capacity across multiple time intervals. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=24) were trained on a simple reward-based task requiring them to remember when the lights would turn off each day. The training consisted of three 30-minute sessions per week over four weeks, with daily interval length increasing from 15-60 minutes. Performance was assessed by measuring latency to enter dark box after lighting change. Results showed that rats quickly learned to make appropriate responses within two trials, even with longer timescales (e.g., 45 minute interval). This study demonstrates that rats are capable of learning complex temporal discrimination tasks, suggesting their cognitive abilities may be significantly underestimated. These findings have implications for behavioral research, understanding animal cognition, and designing more effective methods for studying human memory processes.
outputs.Z: Temporal discrimination learning requires animals to associate specific temporal intervals with reward presentation, yet the temporal resolution and sensitivity of behavioral responses remain poorly characterized. We investigated whether rats could reliably discriminate between 0.5-2.0 s temporal intervals during a single training session using automated operant conditioning techniques. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n=24) were trained in an automated chamber equipped with two response levers and a timed food dispenser that delivered rewards after predetermined time intervals. Animals received fixed-ratio 1 reinforcement schedules for each interval condition: 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, or 2.0 seconds. Learning curves demonstrated rapid acquisition, with most subjects reaching baseline performance within 15 minutes. Discrimination indices showed significant differences across conditions by session 3 (p<0.01), with mean accuracy scores ranging from 84% for 0.5-second intervals to 71% for 2.0-second intervals. title: Synthesis and pharmacological evaluation of 4-phenoxy-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinolines and 4,5,6,6a-tetrahydrochromeno[2,3,4-de]isoquinolines.
reference_abstract: The novel 4-phenoxy-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinolines 6a-c and their rigid congeners 4,5,6,6a-tetrahydro-chromeno[2,3,4-de]isoquinolines 7a,b were synthesized in order to obtain dopamine D2-like receptor ligands. The new compounds were evaluated for their in vitro binding affinities, in vivo behavioral activities on rats, and for their effects on rat brain neurochemistry. Compounds 6b (toward both D2 and D3 dopamine receptors) and 7a,b (toward D3 only dopamine receptors) showed the most significant affinities. However none of the new compounds was able to stimulate behavioral activity in non pre-treated rats, nor to influence brain neurochemistry.

outputs.X: 4-Phenoxy-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline derivatives represent a structurally diverse family of bioactive compounds with potential therapeutic applications in neuropharmacology. We describe the synthesis and pharmacological characterization of a series of 25 novel 4-phenoxytetrahydroisoquinoline analogues (3a-o) and their corresponding 4,5,6,6a-tetrahydrochromeno[2,3,4-de]isoquinoline counterparts (8a-w), both synthesized via modified Pictet-Spengler condensation reactions using commercially available precursors. The target molecules were subjected to comprehensive radioligand binding assays using human dopamine D₂ receptors, rat α₁-adrenergic receptors, and mouse 5-HT₇ receptors. In vitro cellular functional assays evaluated receptor-mediated calcium mobilization pathways and second messenger responses in HEK293 cells stably expressing recombinant G-protein-coupled receptors
outputs.Y: We describe the synthesis and preliminary neuropharmacology of two novel classes of biologically active compounds, 4-phenoxy-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline (TIQ) derivatives and 4,5,6,6a-tetrahydrochromenoo[2,3,4-de]isoquinoline (CHIQT). The TIQ derivatives were synthesized by reaction of 4-phenoxyphenylacetaldehyde with isoquinolinium salts. The synthesis of some novel 4-phenoxy-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline derivatives is described. The chemical properties and the biological activity of these compounds were investigated in vitro (muscarinic acetylcholine receptors binding test) and in vivo (anti-inflammatory effects). We also describe a new route to synthesize 4,5,6,6a-tetrahydrochromeno[2,3,4-de]isoquinoline derivatives by cyclization of dihydropyrazolo[4,3-b]indole. These compounds are very interesting because they present a good anti-inflammatory effect without side effects. title: Psychedelic Psychiatry's Brave New World.
reference_abstract: After a legally mandated, decades-long global arrest of research on psychedelic drugs, investigation of psychedelics in the context of psychiatric disorders is yielding exciting results. Outcomes of neuroscience and clinical research into 5-Hydroxytryptamine 2A (5-HT2A) receptor agonists, such as psilocybin, show promise for addressing a range of serious disorders, including depression and addiction.

outputs.X: In recent years, the medical use of psychedelics has been revitalized. Several studies have shown that psychedelic-assisted psychotherapy is effective in treating psychiatric disorders such as depression and anxiety. The effects are especially remarkable for those who experience severe mental illness or who do not respond to conventional therapies. This article discusses the history of the psychopharmacological revolution and how it relates to the current revival of psychedelic psychiatry. It also outlines some possible mechanisms by which psychedelics may work, including their ability to alter consciousness, disrupt cognitive patterns, affect neurochemistry, and trigger neural changes. Finally, it considers some ethical issues surrounding the therapeutic use of psychedelics. Psychiatric patients deserve better treatment options. Psychedelic therapy could be a game-changer, but there are important questions that need to be addressed before this new field can become part of mainstream medicine. In particular, we must address concerns about safety and efficacy, as well as ensure that these drugs are only used under appropriate conditions.
outputs.Y: The resurgence of psychedelic-assisted psychotherapy has sparked renewed interest in understanding the neuropharmacological mechanisms underlying these compounds' therapeutic effects. While classical psychopharmacology primarily addresses neurotransmitter systems, emerging evidence suggests that psychedelics may interact with complex networks beyond traditional monoaminergic pathways. This review synthesizes preclinical and clinical data from recent decades to examine the neurobiological basis of psychedelic psychiatry's transformative potential. We conducted a comprehensive analysis of published studies investigating serotonin 2A (5-HT₂A) receptor modulation by psychedelics, including LSD, psilocybin, DMT, and mescaline, along with their unique interactions with glutamatergic, GABAergic, and cholinergic systems. Preliminary findings suggest that psychedelics activate novel non-canonical synaptic transmission mechanisms involving polyamine receptors, protein kinase C activation, and calcium-dependent signaling cascades beyond conventional serotonergic pathways. Clinical trials demonstrate significant improvements in treatment-resistant depression, anxiety disorders
outputs.Z: The history of psychedelic psychiatry and psychopharmacology has been an exciting and sometimes controversial story, but it is often difficult to understand the context in which these discoveries were made. The present article reviews some important historical events that have shaped modern psychedelics research and treatment programs. It begins with a brief overview of the early work on LSD conducted by Albert Hofmann at Sandoz Laboratories during the 1950s and 1960s. We then discuss the subsequent efforts to develop safe and effective forms of therapy using psilocybin or other hallucinogens for psychiatric conditions such as depression and anxiety disorders. Finally, we examine how current legal restrictions imposed on clinical studies of psychedelics may limit future progress in this field. The past decade has seen significant advances in understanding the mechanisms underlying psychedelic effects and potential therapeutic applications. These developments provide hope for improving the lives of many people suffering from mental illness through novel treatments that could replace traditional pharmacological approaches. title: Affect recognition deficits in schizophrenia: neural substrates and psychopharmacological implications.
reference_abstract: Impaired emotional functioning is a prominent feature of schizophrenia that includes significant deficits in the ability to accurately recognize facial expressions of emotion. Recent work demonstrates that deficits in affect perception are related to functional outcome and negative symptoms, suggesting that remediation of these deficits may contribute to symptomatic and functional improvements. Thus far, antipsychotic drug treatments appear to be relatively ineffective as a remedial tool. However, investigations into the neurobiology of affective dysfunction show abnormal amygdala activation in schizophrenia during affect recognition tasks. Such findings indicate that regulation of amygdala responses may aid in more accurate emotion processing. Both serotonergic and anxiolytic GABAergic agents that modulate activation of the limbic system offer promising avenues for remediation efforts.

outputs.X: The neurobiologic basis of affect recognition deficits in schizophrenia has yet to be fully characterized, with current understanding limited primarily to behavioral studies. We employed functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) while healthy volunteers and patients with chronic schizophrenia viewed emotional faces expressing positive, negative, or neutral emotions, along with control scenes. Patients demonstrated significantly reduced activation across a broad network involving the amygdala, superior temporal gyrus, inferior frontal cortex, insula, and anterior cingulate cortex compared to controls. Moreover, these abnormalities were positively correlated with degree of social functioning impairment and negatively associated with duration of illness, suggesting that early treatment intervention may modulate affect recognition capacity through alterations in synaptic plasticity mechanisms within key brain regions. Psychotropic medications commonly used for schizophrenia showed differential effects on facial emotion processing; clozapine and olanzapine enhanced amygdala activation during happy face viewing, whereas risperidone and haloperidol decreased activation in frontotemporal areas when presented with angry faces.
outputs.Y: Affective processing deficits are consistently observed in patients with schizophrenia, yet the underlying neurobiological mechanisms remain poorly understood. This study investigated the neural basis of affective recognition impairment using event-related potentials (ERPs) during face-processing tasks in medicated and unmedicated schizophrenic patients compared to healthy controls. Thirty-two treatment-naïve first-episode schizophrenics, 32 chronically-medicated outpatients, and 64 age-matched controls were recruited. Participants viewed positive, neutral, or negative facial expressions while ERPs were recorded from frontal, temporal, and parietal electrodes. Medication effects on affect recognition were evaluated through comparison between medication-free and meditated groups within the schizophrenic population. Control subjects showed typical P300 amplitude enhancement for emotional faces compared to neutral stimuli, with peak latencies of 400-450 ms in both hemispheres. Patients demonstrated significantly reduced P300 amplitude for emotional versus neutral faces across all conditions (p<0.001), with enhanced fronto-temp
outputs.Z:
Schizophrenia is a severe mental disorder characterized by positive symptoms (hallucinations, delusions), negative symptoms (anhedonia) and cognitive impairments. The most common negative symptom of schizophrenia is affective blunting or flattening; however, the pathophysiology of this syndrome remains unknown. Here we review the literature on affect recognition deficits in patients with schizophrenia. We also discuss the neuropharmacological mechanisms underlying these deficits and describe recent findings that suggest the potential utility of drugs targeting different brain regions to treat these deficits. Finally, we provide an overview of the current treatment options for these defects. These results highlight the importance of developing novel treatments aimed at restoring the ability to recognize emotions in individuals with schizophrenia. In addition, this work may help identify the specific biological pathways involved in emotional processing deficits in people suffering from psychosis. title: Data collection and analysis strategies for phMRI.
reference_abstract: Although functional MRI traditionally has been applied mainly to study changes in task-induced brain function, evolving acquisition methodologies and improved knowledge of signal mechanisms have increased the utility of this method for studying responses to pharmacological stimuli, a technique often dubbed "phMRI". The proliferation of higher magnetic field strengths and the use of exogenous contrast agent have boosted detection power, a critical factor for successful phMRI due to the restricted ability to average multiple stimuli within subjects. Receptor-based models of neurovascular coupling, including explicit pharmacological models incorporating receptor densities and affinities and data-driven models that incorporate weak biophysical constraints, have demonstrated compelling descriptions of phMRI signal induced by dopaminergic stimuli. This report describes phMRI acquisition and analysis methodologies, with an emphasis on data-driven analyses. As an example application, statistically efficient data-driven regressors were used to describe the biphasic response to the mu-opioid agonist remifentanil, and antagonism using dopaminergic and GABAergic ligands revealed modulation of the mesolimbic pathway. Results illustrate the power of phMRI as well as our incomplete understanding of mechanisms underlying the signal. Future directions are discussed for phMRI acquisitions in human studies, for evolving analysis methodologies, and for interpretative studies using the new generation of simultaneous PET/MRI scanners. This article is part of the Special Issue Section entitled 'Neuroimaging in Neuropharmacology'.

outputs.X:
Pharmacological magnetic resonance imaging (phMRI) is a valuable tool for investigating the neuropharmacology of psychiatric disorders, but it faces many challenges in terms of data acquisition, processing, and interpretation. In this review we provide an overview of the technical challenges associated with phMRI, and describe how recent advances in hardware and software have improved its utility as a research tool. We discuss key methods for designing experimental protocols, collecting functional MRI (fMRI) data, and analyzing them, including techniques for optimizing fMRI signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), correcting for physiological noise, detecting changes in neural activity that are too small to be observed by standard statistical analyses, and combining results from different subjects or tasks. Finally, we outline current trends in phMRI research, such as the use of machine learning algorithms, and highlight future directions for the field. This article is part of the Special Issue Section entitled 'Neuroimaging of Psychopharmacologic Effects.' ### Highlights:

outputs.Y: Functional MRI (fMRI) studies of behavior have become increasingly popular, but methods for collecting and analyzing fMRI data remain poorly standardized. We developed a protocol for optimal data acquisition in behavioral fMRI research with specific attention to signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), time resolution, and spatial coverage. We used rat models to study visual perception, motor learning, and pain processing using high-field fMRI at 7T. The protocol included the following components: multiple-slice multi-echo EPI sequences with slice thicknesses ranging from 0.5 mm to 2 mm depending on the task, gradient echo timing parameters optimized for maximum SNR, and whole-brain coverage across all tasks. Data were analyzed using standard fMRI tools including SPM99 and custom scripts written in Matlab. This protocol resulted in significantly improved SNR compared to previously published protocols, allowing detection of activation patterns within single voxels that had not been detectable before. outputs.Z: Functional magnetic resonance imaging of the brain (fMRI) has emerged as a powerful neuroimaging technique with increasing clinical applications, yet standardized data acquisition protocols and analytic approaches remain inconsistently applied across laboratories. We conducted a comprehensive review of current fMRI practice patterns among 78 academic research centers worldwide, analyzing both acquisition parameters and statistical analysis methods used in published studies from 2000-2012. Survey responses were analyzed using chi-square tests to examine protocol consistency across different methodologies and study designs. Statistical analyses employed hierarchical clustering algorithms to identify common data processing workflows. Results demonstrated significant variability in functional sequence design, with only 37% of institutions consistently employing EPI-based acquisitions and 28% utilizing gradient echo sequences. Acquisition durations varied widely between 5-20 minutes per volume, while temporal resolution ranged from 1.5-3 seconds.
